CON.title: Constitutional Law — Essay Rules
checklist: Justiciability first: standing (individual → organizational) · mootness · ripeness · political question
Legislative powers: Commerce Clause (channels · instrumentalities · substantial effect/aggregation) · spending power (+ separate commandeering IRAC) · delegation
Executive power: executive orders — domestic vs. foreign affairs
State powers: Supremacy Clause → preemption (express/implied) · Privileges and Immunities · 10th Am commandeering · Dormant Commerce Clause (discriminatory vs. undue burden)
Takings: government action → private property → public use → physical vs. regulatory (total/partial) → just compensation
Due process: procedural (amount of process due) · substantive (fundamental right → strict scrutiny; else rational basis) — often tested together
Equal protection: discrimination + intent → pick scrutiny (suspect/fundamental · gender/nonmarital children · everything else)
Free speech: overbreadth · vagueness → prior restraint · unfettered discretion → content-based vs. content-neutral → forum → lesser/unprotected speech (commercial, obscenity)
Religion: Establishment Clause (facial preference?) · Free Exercise (belief vs. conduct; neutral laws)

KTR.title: Contracts and Sales — Essay Rules
checklist: Applicable law: UCC (sale of goods) vs. common law (services) → mixed contract: predominant purpose → merchant status

Formation: offer (bilateral/unilateral, irrevocable, termination) → acceptance (manner, mirror image / UCC 2-207, mailbox) → consideration (detriment, gift, preexisting duty, past consideration, promissory estoppel)
Defenses to formation: mistake (mutual/unilateral) · misrepresentation · undue influence · duress · capacity
Defenses to enforcement: illegality · unconscionability · statute of frauds → exceptions
Terms: modification (CL/UCC) → accord & satisfaction → parol evidence rule → integration → exceptions
Performance: promise vs. condition → conditions (express/implied, precedent/subsequent, excuse) → discharge (impracticability, impossibility, frustration) → warranties (sale of goods)
Breach: anticipatory repudiation (CL/UCC, assurances, retraction) → material vs. minor → perfect tender → right to cure → F.O.B. shipment/destination
Remedies: expectation → consequential → reliance → incidental → mitigation → restitution / quasi-contract → specific performance (laches, unclean hands) → UCC limitation of remedies, risk of loss

CRL.title: Criminal Law — Essay Rules
checklist: Structure chronologically — address the crimes as they occur in the facts; Criminal Law is usually tested with Criminal Procedure
Homicide order: common-law murder (malice quartet: intent to kill · intent to inflict GBI · depraved heart · felony murder) → 1st degree → 2nd degree → voluntary → involuntary manslaughter
Felony murder: separate inherently dangerous felony → its elements → foreseeability → proximate cause → limitations (bystander · cofelon/Redline · point of safety)
Defenses (only if the facts raise them, typically in homicide): insanity (M'Naghten · irresistible impulse · Durham · MPC) · intoxication · self-defense · duress · entrapment
Crimes against the person: battery · kidnapping
Theft crimes: larceny · embezzlement · robbery · burglary · receipt of stolen property — separate IRAC for each, merger at the end
Party liability: accomplice (aid/abet + intent → natural and probable consequences) · accessory after the fact
Inchoate: solicitation (merges) · conspiracy (NO merger; withdrawal rules) · attempt (merges)

CRP.title: Criminal Procedure — Essay Rules
checklist: 4th Am: government conduct → standing/REP → seizure of the person (arrest, Terry stop, checkpoint, traffic stop) → search
Warrant: validity (magistrate, probable cause, particularity) → execution → good faith
No/invalid warrant: stop & frisk · search incident to arrest · automobile · plain view · exigency · consent — discuss every exception the facts raise
Close every 4th Am analysis: exclusionary rule → fruit of the poisonous tree → exceptions
5th Am: Miranda (custody + interrogation) → waiver/invocation → exclusion of statements → involuntary confessions
6th Am: right to counsel — attachment, offense-specific, lineups
Due process: unnecessarily suggestive lineups
Rarely tested: Confrontation Clause, guilty pleas, discharging counsel, self-representation, severance, speedy trial, right to testify

TOR.title: Torts — Essay Rules
checklist: Find all the torts — be aggressive with the checklist: negligence → strict liability → products liability → defamation → invasion of privacy → intentional torts → vicarious liability → immunity

Negligence: duty (who is owed — Cardozo/Andrews; standard of care) → breach → negligence per se → causation (actual → proximate → intervening/superseding) → damages → defenses (contributory/comparative, assumption of risk)
Strict liability: abnormally dangerous activity → wild/domestic animals → causation → damages → defenses
Products liability: strict (absolute duty → defect: manufacturing/design/failure to warn → causation → damages → defenses) → negligence → warranties (express, merchantability, fitness)
Defamation: defamatory language → of/concerning P → publication → reputation damages (libel/slander) → constitutional requirements (falsity, fault) → defenses (truth, consent, privileges)
Invasion of privacy: misappropriation · intrusion upon seclusion · false light · public disclosure of private facts

Intentional torts: battery · assault · IIED · false imprisonment · trespass to chattels · conversion · trespass to land · nuisance → defenses (consent, self-defense/others, shopkeeper's privilege, necessity)
Vicarious liability: employee vs. independent contractor → scope of employment → nondelegable duty / inherently dangerous → immunity (largely eliminated)
Rarely tested: res ipsa loquitur

CIV.title: Civil Procedure — Essay Rules
checklist: SMJ: federal question → diversity (complete diversity + $75k) → supplemental → removal/remand
PJ: traditional bases → long-arm → minimum contacts (purposeful availment, foreseeability, relatedness: specific/general) → fairness
Venue: proper in original district? → transfer analysis → CA venue rules (property, contract, tort)
Erie: federal question → federal law · diversity → outcome-determinative state law is substantive · CA conflict-of-law rules
Pleadings: service → complaint → 12(b) motions / CA demurrer → answer → amendments + relation back → Rule 11
Joinder: claims (Rule 18) · counterclaims/cross-claims · parties (necessary/indispensable, permissive) · intervention · interpleader · impleader
Discovery: scope/relevance → privileges (attorney-client, work product, physician-patient) → tools (depositions, interrogatories, production, exams, admissions) → compel → sanctions
Trial: jury right + demand → JMOL (CA nonsuit) → JNOV → appeals (final judgment rule)
Preclusion: claim preclusion (CA primary rights) · issue preclusion (mutuality/fairness) — often via summary judgment
CA distinctions tested on past exams: choice of law, claim preclusion, venue — flag "In California…" throughout

EVI.title: Evidence — Essay Rules
checklist: Every CA Evidence essay: open with the Prop 8 + CEC 352 paragraph (civil → add "Prop 8 will not be applied"); if the call is silent, use the FRE
Every item of evidence: logical relevance → legal relevance
Form of question: leading · answer gives information not asked for · assumes facts not in evidence · calls for narrative
Character: general bar → civil (at issue) → criminal (D's character, victim's character, opening the door) → prior bad acts/MIMIC → impeachment
Witnesses: competence → lay/expert opinion → recollection refreshed/recorded → impeachment (seven methods) → rehabilitation
Tangible evidence: authentication → best evidence rule
Privileges: spousal testimonial · marital communications · attorney-client · physician/psychotherapist · public-policy exclusions
Hearsay: definition → assertive conduct → nonhearsay use → double hearsay → exemptions (prior statements, party-opponent) → exceptions (declarant unavailable / availability immaterial / residual)
CA distinctions: Prop 8/CEC 352 · spousal testimony privilege · spontaneous & contemporaneous statement · medical diagnosis/treatment

PRF.title: Professional Responsibility — Essay Rules
checklist: Organize by duty or by fact; discuss each duty in its own IRAC paragraph, reusing facts as needed
Lawyer-client relationship: formation → scope (settlement, strategy, crime/fraud) → termination (mandatory vs permissive withdrawal, MR vs CA)
Fees (MR reasonable vs CA not unconscionable): writing → contingent fees → fee splitting → referrals → client trust accounts
Privilege & confidentiality: lawyer-client privilege → work product → duty of confidentiality → exceptions (all MR vs CA)
Conflicts (analyze each separately): lawyer-client → current clients → former clients → prospective clients → third-party payment → organization as client
Competence and diligence
Litigation: frivolous claims → candor → fairness → client/witness perjury → communications → trial publicity → prosecutor duties
Advertising and solicitation → integrity: reporting misconduct → unauthorized practice
Frequently tested CA distinctions: privilege and confidentiality exceptions · false testimony · fees (amount, referrals, splitting)

REA.title: Real Property — Essay Rules
checklist: Approach chronologically or by party — address each party's property interest separately
Ownership: present estates → restraints on alienation → joint tenancy (four unities) → severance → cotenant rights (ouster, rents, expenses, repairs)
Landlord-tenant: leasehold type → tenant duties → landlord duties (habitability, quiet enjoyment, constructive eviction) → assignment/sublease
Land-sales contract: marketable title → merger; deeds: validity → delivery intent → recording acts (notice, race, race-notice) → BFP/shelter
Easements: classification → type → scope → transfer → termination (sale to BFP)
Covenants vs equitable servitudes: writing → intent → touch and concern → notice → privity (real covenants only) → damages vs injunction
Mortgages and deeds of trust → purchase-money priority → foreclosure priority (first in time; recording acts)
Takings is a listed common fact pattern; this workshop states no takings rules

WIL.title: Wills — Essay Rules
checklist: Intestacy: spouse qualification (married + 120 hours) → CP share (100%) → SP share (100% / one-half / one-third) → issue: per capita with representation
Execution: capacity → testamentary intent → attested will → interested witness → substantial compliance → holographic will → codicils → will substitute (deed) → choice of law
Revocation: subsequent instrument · physical destruction · operation of law (divorce) → revocation of codicil
Revival: republication → dependent relative revocation (mistake of law or fact)
Construction: integration → incorporation by reference → acts of independent significance
Distribution: classify gifts (specific/general/demonstrative/residuary) → abatement → ademption → lapse/anti-lapse → ambiguities
Power to transfer: CP/quasi-CP at death → omitted spouse → omitted children
Contests: insane delusion → undue influence (traditional · CA factors · confidential relationship · statutory presumptions) → fraud
Always: discuss distribution under the will (if valid) AND under intestacy (if invalid)

CMP.title: Community Property — Essay Rules
checklist: Open with the general-principles paragraph (add quasi-community property only if relevant) → status of the parties (married, unmarried, putative spouse) → premarital agreements

For each item of property: presumptions → source (trace every change in form/identity) → actions changing character (transmutation, title, management, fiduciary duties) → specific-property rules → distribution at divorce/separation/death
Specific property: separate-property businesses (Pereira/Van Camp) · improvements · credit and loans · commingled accounts · education degrees · personal-injury recovery · stock options
For each debt: community-property and separate-property liability → reimbursement → assignment at divorce → special debts (necessaries, child/spousal support, torts)
Other property rules in the outline if raised: goodwill, retirement pensions, disability and severance pay, insurance

CORP.title: Corporations — Essay Rules
checklist: Formation: promoter liability (novation / adoption) → incorporation & de jure status → ultra vires → de facto corporation & corporation by estoppel
Securities: board authorizes issuance → adequate consideration → par value rules
Shareholders: annual meeting → voting (elect/remove directors, fundamental changes) → proxies → voting agreements → derivative actions
Piercing the corporate veil (frequently tested) → controlling shareholder's fiduciary duty
Board: selection / removal / quorum → duty of care + business judgment rule → duty of loyalty (self-dealing + safe harbors, usurpation)
Officers: authority via agency law — express, implied, apparent
Dissolution → winding up → distribution order
Watch for professional responsibility crossover: conflicts of interest (lawyer for corporation advising a director/shareholder)

AGP.title: Agency & Partnerships — Essay Rules
checklist: Agency: creation (principal, agent, control) → contractual liability: actual authority (express → implied/by position) → apparent authority → ratification
Agency tort liability: respondeat superior (scope of employment, intentional torts, travel) → direct liability (authorize/ratify, negligent hiring or supervision, nondelegable duty)
Partnership formation: 2+ persons, for-profit, co-owners → profit sharing presumption → no intent or writing required
Between partners: partner as agent → fiduciary duties: loyalty (usurping opportunity) · care → profits and losses → management rights
Third parties: partner's authority (express/implied/apparent) → partnership liability for partner's torts → joint and several liability → exhaust partnership assets first
Dissolution and winding up: partnership at will → creditor priority over partners
Rarely tested: LLPs (state filing; limited partners not personally liable)

REM.title: Remedies — Essay Rules
checklist: Attack order: cause(s) of action → legal remedies (money damages) + limitations → equitable remedies + equitable defenses → restitution-based remedies (legal and equitable) + defenses

Causes of action paired with Remedies: contracts (anticipatory repudiation / breach — land, goods, construction, employment, mistake) · torts (trespass, conversion, nuisance, misappropriation of money, personal injury, fraud)
Money damages: compensatory (expectation + consequential + incidental) → nominal · reliance · punitive · restitution · liquidated
Limitations on every damages type: actual/proximate causation, certainty, avoidable consequences (duty to mitigate)
Equitable: injunctions (TRO → preliminary → permanent) · specific performance · rescission · reformation — close with laches and unclean hands
Restitution: money damages → constructive trust / equitable lien (tracing, BFP cutoff) — close with laches and unclean hands
Don't forget: replevin (torts and contracts) · ejectment (torts)

TRU.title: Trusts — Essay Rules
checklist: Parties: settlor → trustee → beneficiaries
Express trust: intent → trust property (res) → valid purpose → ascertainable beneficiaries; inter vivos vs testamentary; omitted children
Validity challenges: undue influence (traditional four elements + confidential-relationship presumption) → fraud (inducement, execution)
Charitable trusts: charitable purpose → indefinite beneficiaries → cy pres (specific vs general intent)
Distribution rights: income/remainder → lapse and anti-lapse → discretionary/mandatory → alienability → support and spendthrift trusts
Termination: revocable vs irrevocable (CA presumes revocable) → purpose accomplished / beneficiary consent / court → trustee removal
Trustee duties: care (follow directions, prudent investor, diversify, productive, impartial) → loyalty (self-dealing) → disclose/account → remedies

core: (1) injury in fact
(2) causation
(3) redressability
bare: (1) i i f
rules.prose: Individual standing requires an injury in fact, causation, and redressability.
rules.name: Lujan v. Defenders of Wildlife
rules.subject: Constitutional Law

core: (1) the individual members have standing
(2) the claim is related to the purpose of the organization
(3) the individual members are not necessary to adjudicate the claim
bare: (1) t i m h s
(2) t c i r t t p o t o
(3) t i m a n n t a t c
guided: (1) the i___ m___ have s___
(2) the c___ is r___ to the p___ of the o___
(3) the i___ m___ are not n___ to a___ the c___
rules.prose: An organization has standing if its individual members have standing, the claim is related to the purpose of the organization, and the individual members are not necessary to adjudicate the claim.
rules.tip: In addressing organizational standing, first analyze individual standing to meet the first requirement.
rules.name: Hunt v. Washington State Apple Advertising Comm'n
rules.subject: Constitutional Law

core: Moot: no live controversy because the claim has already been adjudicated or the harm no longer exists.

rules.subject: Constitutional Law

rules.prompt: Commerce Clause — three categories Congress can regulate
core: Congress has the power to regulate interstate commerce:
(1) the CHANNELS of interstate commerce (highways, waterways)
(2) the INSTRUMENTALITIES of interstate commerce (planes, trains, and automobiles)
(3) activities that have a SUBSTANTIAL EFFECT on interstate commerce
Substantial effect: economic activity may be considered in the AGGREGATE.
bare: C h t p t r i c:
(1) t C o i c (h, w)
(2) t I o i c (p, t, a a)
(3) a t h a S E o i c

rules.prose: Congress has the power to regulate the channels of interstate commerce, the instrumentalities of interstate commerce, and activities that have a substantial effect on interstate commerce. In determining substantial effect, economic activity may be considered in the aggregate.
rules.name: United States v. Lopez
rules.subject: Constitutional Law

core: – Congress can condition federal funds and require states to implement certain regulations
– Permitted as long as the condition is RELATED to the purpose of the funds
bare: – C c c f f a r s t i c r
– P a l a t c i R t t p o t f

rules.prose: Congress can condition federal funds and require states to implement certain regulations, and this is permitted as long as the condition is related to the purpose of the funds.
rules.tip: Valid spending-power conditions are not commandeering — but if the spending power is at issue, consider a separate IRAC explaining why commandeering is or is not an issue.
rules.name: South Dakota v. Dole
rules.subject: Constitutional Law

core: Congress may delegate its legislative power to an agency.

guided: C___ may d___ its l___ p___ to an a___.
rules.subject: Constitutional Law

rules.prompt: Executive orders — domestic and foreign powers
core: The president has the power to issue executive orders within the scope of his presidential powers.
– Domestic affairs: appointment and removal powers, the pardon power, the commander-in-chief power, and the duty to execute the law
– Foreign affairs: conduct foreign negotiations, deploy troops overseas, and make executive agreements

– D a: a a r p, t p p, t c-i-c p, a t d t e t l
– F a: c f n, d t o, a m e a

rules.prose: The president has the power to issue executive orders within the scope of his presidential powers. In domestic affairs, the president has appointment and removal powers, the pardon power, the commander-in-chief power, and the duty to execute the law. In foreign affairs, the president has the power to conduct foreign negotiations, deploy troops overseas, and make executive agreements.
rules.subject: Constitutional Law

core: If state law conflicts with federal law, the federal law governs.
bare: I s l c w f l, t f l g.
rules.subject: Constitutional Law

core: – Express: the federal law explicitly states that it is the only law allowed in that area
– Implied: Congress intends to "occupy the field," OR the state law directly or indirectly conflicts with the federal law
– A state CAN pass more stringent laws than the federal law, so long as they do not conflict
bare: – E: t f l e s t i i t o l a i t a
– I: C i t "o t f," O t s l d o i c w t f l
– A s C p m s l t t f l, s l a t d n c
guided: – E___: the f___ l___ e___ s___ that it is the only l___ a___ in that a___
– I___: C___ i___ to "o___ the f___," OR the s___ l___ d___ or i___ c___ with the f___ l___
– A s___ CAN p___ m___ s___ l___ than the f___ l___, so l___ as they do not c___
rules.prose: Express preemption occurs when the federal law explicitly states that it is the only law allowed in that area. Implied preemption occurs when Congress passes a federal law intending to occupy the field, or when the state law directly or indirectly conflicts with federal law. A state can pass more stringent laws than the federal law, so long as they do not conflict.
rules.subject: Constitutional Law

core: – Prohibits states from discriminating against citizens of OTHER states
– Exception: a state can discriminate against out-of-state citizens if there is a SUBSTANTIAL JUSTIFICATION for the discrimination
– Individuals only: corporations and noncitizens cannot claim this clause
bare: – P s f d a c o O s
– E: a s c d a o-o-s c i t i a S J f t d
– I o: c a n c c t c

rules.prose: The Privileges and Immunities Clause prohibits states from discriminating against citizens of other states. A state can discriminate against out-of-state citizens if there is a substantial justification for the discrimination. This protection only applies to individuals; corporations and noncitizens cannot claim this clause.
rules.subject: Constitutional Law

core: – All powers not expressly given to the federal government are reserved to the states
– The federal government cannot "commandeer" state legislatures and force them to pass laws
bare: – A p n e g t t f g a r t t s
– T f g c "c" s l a f t t p l
guided: – All p___ not e___ g___ to the f___ g___ are r___ to the s___

rules.prose: Under the Tenth Amendment, all powers not expressly given to the federal government are reserved to the states, and the federal government cannot commandeer state legislatures and force them to pass laws.
rules.tip: Often arises with the spending power — the government cannot force a state legislature to enact laws, but it can condition federal funds to persuade the state legislature.
rules.name: New York v. United States
rules.subject: Constitutional Law

core: The Commerce Clause enables Congress to regulate interstate commerce.
The Dormant Commerce Clause limits the STATES' ability to regulate interstate commerce, an area that Congress already regulates.

rules.prompt: Dormant Commerce Clause — discriminatory state law
core: If the state law discriminates against out-of-state commerce, the state must show:
(1) it has an important state interest, and
(2) there is no other nondiscriminatory means available to achieve that interest
Exception: market participant.

rules.prose: If the state law is discriminatory against out-of-state commerce, the state must show that it has an important state interest and that there is no other nondiscriminatory means available to achieve that interest, subject to the market-participant exception.
rules.subject: Constitutional Law
rules.area: State Regulation and Taxation of Commerce

Balance: the purpose of the statute vs. the burden on interstate commerce, and whether less restrictive alternatives exist.

rules.name: Pike balancing (Pike v. Bruce Church)
rules.subject: Constitutional Law
rules.area: State Regulation and Taxation of Commerce

core: The government may not take private property for public use without just compensation.
– Government action: federal, state, or local government taking property (physically taking, rezoning, prohibiting development)
– Private property: usually land, but can be other property
– Public use: includes health, safety, economic development, etc.
bare: T g m n t p p f p u w j c.
– G a: f, s, o l g t p (p t, r, p d)
– P p: u l, b c b o p
– P u: i h, s, e d, e.
guided: The g___ may not t___ p___ p___ for p___ u___ without j___ c___.
– P___ p___: u___ l___, but can be o___ p___
rules.prose: Under the Fifth Amendment Takings Clause, the government may not take private property for public use without just compensation. The taking may be by the federal, state, or local government, whether by physically taking, rezoning, or prohibiting development; the property is usually land but can be other property; and the property must be taken for public use, which includes health, safety, and economic development.
rules.name: Kelo v. City of New London
rules.subject: Constitutional Law

rules.prompt: Types of taking — physical vs. regulatory (total or partial)
core: – Physical taking: the government physically occupies the land
– Regulatory taking: a law has the effect of DECREASING THE VALUE of the owner's property
– Total: the regulation leaves NO economically viable use of the property
– Partial: the regulation affects some economic use of the land, but some economic use remains
bare: – P t: t g p o t l
– R t: a l h t e o D T V o t o p
– T: t r l N e v u o t p
– P: t r a s e u o t l, b s e u r
guided: – R___ t___: a l___ has the e___ of D___ THE V___ of the o___ p___
– T___: the r___ l___ NO e___ v___ u___ of the p___
rules.prose: A physical taking occurs when the government physically occupies the land. A regulatory taking occurs when a law has the effect of decreasing the value of the owner's property; it is total when the regulation leaves no economically viable use of the property, and partial when the regulation affects some economic use of the land but some economic use is still available.
rules.name: Lucas v. South Carolina Coastal Council
rules.subject: Constitutional Law

core: (1) economic impact: how much value was lost due to the regulation?
(2) reasonable expectations: the owner's reasonable expectation of return on investment
(3) character of the regulation: does it impact a few owners or the entire community?

(3) c o t r: d i i a f o o t e c?
guided: (3) c___ of the r___: does it i___ a f___ o___ or the e___ c___?
rules.prose: For a partial regulatory taking, courts consider the economic impact, meaning how much value was lost due to the regulation; the owner's reasonable expectation of return on investment; and the character of the regulation, meaning whether the regulation impacts a few owners or the entire community.
rules.name: Penn Central factors
rules.subject: Constitutional Law

(examples of procedures: notice, hearings, appeals, a neutral decision-maker)

rules.tip: The Due Process Clause includes both procedural and substantive due process — these concepts are often tested together.
rules.subject: Constitutional Law

rules.prompt: Amount of process due — three factors
core: (1) the interest affected — life, liberty (including fundamental rights), or property
(2) the value of additional safeguards
(3) the burden/cost of additional process
bare: (1) t i a — l, l (i f r), o p

rules.prose: The amount of process due depends on the interest affected, whether life, liberty, or property; the value of additional safeguards; and the burden or cost of the additional process.
rules.name: Mathews v. Eldridge
rules.subject: Constitutional Law

rules.prompt: Substantive due process — rule and standards of review
core: Substantive due process: whether the government's action (a law or regulation) impermissibly infringes on an individual's rights.
– Fundamental rights (life, liberty, and property) → strict scrutiny: valid only if NECESSARY to achieve a COMPELLING governmental interest
– Nonfundamental rights → rational basis: valid if rationally related to a legitimate government interest
bare: S d p: w t g a (a l o r) i i o a i r.
– F r (l, l, a p) → s s: v o i N t a a C g i
– N r → r b: v i r r t a l g i
rules.prose: Substantive due process asks whether the government's action impermissibly infringes on an individual's rights. Laws affecting fundamental rights receive strict scrutiny and are only valid if necessary to achieve a compelling governmental interest; laws affecting nonfundamental rights receive rational basis review and are valid if rationally related to a legitimate government interest.
rules.subject: Constitutional Law

core: – The plaintiff must show discrimination
– To apply a stricter standard of review, the plaintiff must show DISCRIMINATORY INTENT

– T a a s s o r, t p m s D I

rules.prose: The plaintiff must show discrimination, and in order to apply a stricter standard of review, the plaintiff must show discriminatory intent.
rules.tip: Look for the government treating classes of people differently — the law in the fact pattern will often be facially discriminatory, favoring one group over another.

core: – Fundamental right or suspect classification (race, ethnicity, national origin) → strict scrutiny
– Gender or nonmarital children → intermediate scrutiny
– All other rights and classifications → rational basis

rules.prose: Strict scrutiny applies to fundamental rights and suspect classifications, which are race, ethnicity, and national origin. Intermediate scrutiny applies to gender and nonmarital children. All other rights and classifications receive rational basis review.
rules.tip: If the classification involves more than one category, different provisions of the statute may be evaluated under different levels of scrutiny.

– valid if viewpoint-neutral and reasonably related to a legitimate government interest
– (can ban all war speech (content); cannot ban only prowar/antiwar speech (viewpoint))

rules.prose: In a traditional public forum, such as sidewalks, streets, and parks, or a designated public forum, content-based regulations are subject to strict scrutiny and content-neutral regulations are subject to the time, place, and manner test. The government can regulate speech in a nonpublic forum — such as government offices, schools, jails, military bases, and airport terminals — if the regulation is viewpoint-neutral and reasonably related to a legitimate government interest.
rules.subject: Constitutional Law

core: Prohibits the government from establishing a religion, preferring a particular religion over another, or preferring religion over nonreligion.
– Facially religious preference → strict scrutiny
– No facial preference (the statute's impact favors religion) → the "historical practices and understanding" test (a fact-based analysis)

– N f p (t s i f r) → t "h p a u" t (a f-b a)
rules.prose: The Establishment Clause prohibits the government from establishing a religion, preferring a particular religion over another, or preferring religion over nonreligion. When a law facially shows a preference for one religion over another, or for religion over nonreligion, strict scrutiny applies. When a statute has no facial preference but has an impact of favoring religion, apply the historical practices and understanding test, which is a fact-based analysis.
rules.name: Kennedy v. Bremerton (praying coach case)
rules.subject: Constitutional Law

– Neutral laws that do not target religion and are generally applicable → rational basis

rules.prose: The Free Exercise Clause prohibits the government from interfering with a person's religious belief or conduct. The religion must be genuine, and a person's religious beliefs are absolutely protected. Laws that intentionally target religious conduct are subject to strict scrutiny, while neutral laws that do not target religion and are generally applicable are subject to the rational basis test.

rules.prose: Private conduct will not trigger constitutional guarantees, except the Thirteenth Amendment's prohibition of slavery; state action includes actions by federal as well as local governments. Private conduct is treated as state action when a traditional government function is performed by private persons, or when there is significant state involvement with the private conduct, such that the government is pervasively entwined with the private party's conduct.

– (2) injunctive relief against a state official
– (3) money damages against a state officer personally, not paid by the state treasury
– (4) congressional abrogation under the 13th, 14th, and 15th Amendments (Civil War Amendments)

– (2) there is a special relationship between the plaintiff and the third party, or
– (3) the injury to the plaintiff affects the relationship with the third party

rules.prose: Taxpayer standing is very limited: a taxpayer cannot challenge government expenditures generally unless challenging a specific legislatively authorized expenditure as a violation of the Establishment Clause. A plaintiff has no standing to assert a third party's rights unless the third party is unable to assert her own rights, there is a special relationship between the plaintiff and the third party, or the injury to the plaintiff affects the relationship with the third party.

rules.subject: Contracts

rules.prose: Offers are generally revocable. Under the common law, an option contract is an offer where the offeror promises to hold the offer open for a certain period of time; the offeree must pay consideration to hold the offer open. Under the UCC, a merchant's firm offer is an offer in writing where the offeror, who must be a merchant, promises to hold the offer open for a certain period of time that cannot exceed 90 days; no consideration is required.

rules.prose: Under UCC 2-207, an acceptance with changes or additions is a valid acceptance. If any party is a non-merchant, the contract will not include the changes unless the offeror agrees to them. If both parties are merchants, the contract will include the changes unless they materially alter the terms of the original offer, the original offer limits acceptance to the terms of the offer, or the offeror has previously objected or objects to the changed or new terms.
rules.tip: Terms that materially alter a contract include a warranty disclaimer or a severe limitation of remedies. Terms that do not: setting a reasonable time for a party to complain about the goods, or reasonable limitations on remedies.
rules.subject: Contracts

core: An acceptance is valid when placed in the mail.
Exception: option contract or firm offer — acceptance is valid when received, and must be received before the offer expires.

rules.subject: Contracts

core: Fraudulent: intentional misrepresentation of fact, justifiably relied on — affirmative (a lie) or non-disclosure.

– Fraud in the inducement: induces the contract → voidable by the adversely affected party who justifiably relied

rules.prose: A fraudulent misrepresentation is an intentional misrepresentation of a fact that the innocent party justifiably relies on; it can be affirmative or through non-disclosure. If it prevents a party from knowing the character or essential terms of the transaction (fraud in the factum), no contract is formed and the apparent contract is void. If it is used to induce another to enter into a contract (fraud in the inducement), the contract is voidable by the adversely affected party if she justifiably relied on the misrepresentation in entering into the agreement.

rules.prose: A court will not enforce a contract that is so unfair that no reasonable person would agree to it. Procedural unconscionability means the bargaining process leading to the formation of the contract was unfair, such as where a party in a superior position takes advantage of that position. Substantive unconscionability means the actual terms of the contract are unfair. If a court finds unconscionability, it can refuse to enforce the entire contract, strike the unconscionable portion, or limit the unconscionable terms.

rules.prose: The Statute of Frauds applies to contracts involving marriage, suretyship, contracts — usually for services — that cannot be performed within one year, the sale of goods for $500 or more, and real property.

rules.prose: There must be a writing signed by the person to be charged — the person against whom enforcement is sought — that contains the essential terms of the deal. The writing does not have to be a formal contract; it can be in the form of letters or receipts, and multiple writings can be put together to meet the requirements as long as they reference each other. The writing does not have to exist at the time of the promise; it can be created after the promises are made and still meet the SOF.

rules.subject: Contracts

rules.prompt: SOF exceptions — UCC goods $500 or more (four)

– Part performance (part of the purchase price paid) → enforceable to the extent of the money paid
– Specially manufactured goods for the buyer → no writing required
– Confirmatory memo: both merchants; receiver knowingly receives it and does not respond within 10 days → enforceable against receiver even unsigned

rules.prose: For a sale of goods of $500 or more, if full performance has occurred by the party seeking to enforce, the contract is fully enforceable; if part of the purchase price has been paid, it is enforceable to the extent of the money paid. No writing is required if the contract involves specially manufactured goods for the buyer. Where both parties are merchants and one sends a confirmatory memo that the other knowingly receives and does not respond to within 10 days, the contract is enforceable against the receiving party even if it did not sign the memo.

– SOF: a modified contract within the statute must be in writing (unless an exception applies)

rules.prose: Any change to the terms of a valid contract is a modification, and both parties must agree to it. At common law, a modification must be supported by consideration, and a provision requiring a modification to be in writing even though it would not otherwise fall within the Statute of Frauds is not enforceable. Under the UCC, a modification does not require additional consideration as long as it is entered into in good faith by both parties, and provisions prohibiting oral modifications are valid. If the modified contract falls within the statute of frauds, it must be in writing unless an exception applies.
rules.subject: Contracts

core: – Accord: a new agreement to accept a different performance (where a dispute exists over the contract's validity or the amount owed)
– Satisfaction: completion of the different performance — discharges the original contract duties and the accord duties
– Consideration: the performing party incurs a legal detriment; the accepting party gives up the right to dispute the original contract and sue

rules.prose: If there is a dispute over the validity of the contract or the amount owed, a party can agree to accept a different performance than what was agreed upon; this new agreement is the accord. The satisfaction occurs when the different performance is completed, which discharges the original contract duties and the accord agreement duties. Consideration supports the agreement because the party performing the different performance is incurring a legal detriment, and the party agreeing to accept it is giving up the right to dispute the original contract and sue for breach.
rules.subject: Contracts

– Merger clause ("final and complete understanding") → likely a total integration

rules.prose: A court first asks whether the writing was intended to be the final agreement; if the contract is not an integration, the parol evidence rule does not apply. If the writing contains all of the terms of the agreement, it is a total integration and no parol evidence is admissible. If it contains some of the terms, it is a partial integration, and parol evidence is admissible as long as it is consistent with the writing. A merger clause stating that the contract is the final and complete understanding of the parties makes a total integration likely.
rules.subject: Contracts

core: – Express: written in the contract itself ("on condition that")
– Implied: not written, but a court may find one (e.g., builder must substantially perform before the owner's duty to pay)

Implied conditions are not written in the contract, but a court may find that an implied condition exists — for example, a court may imply that a builder has to substantially perform before the owner has a duty to pay.
rules.subject: Contracts

core: – Condition precedent: the condition must occur before the other party has an obligation to perform
– Condition subsequent: if the condition occurs, the duty to perform is then excused

rules.subject: Contracts

core: – Express conditions must be met fully
– Implied conditions require substantial performance
– A condition that is not met → the other party's duty to perform is excused completely

rules.prose: Express conditions must be met fully, while implied conditions require substantial performance. If a condition is not met, the other party's duty to perform is excused completely.
rules.subject: Contracts

core: – Waiver: by words or conduct, if the condition is not material; the waiving party then has a duty to perform
– Wrongful interference: a party hinders the other's performance and the condition's occurrence → condition excused; wrongful party must perform
– Estoppel: a party indicates it will not enforce the condition + the other reasonably relies → estopped from enforcing it
bare: – W: b w o c, i t c i n m; t w p t h a d t p
– W i: a p h t o p a t c o → c e; w p m p
– E: a p i i w n e t c + t o r r → e f e i

– E___: a p___ i___ it will not e___ the c___ + the o___ r___ r___ → e___ from e___ it
rules.prose: A party can waive a condition by words or conduct, as long as the condition is not material to the contract; the waiving party then has a duty to perform because it waived the condition. If a party hinders the other party's performance and interferes with the occurrence of the condition, the condition will be excused and the wrongful party will have a duty to perform. If a party indicates that it will not enforce a condition and the other party reasonably relies on this, the party will be estopped from later enforcing the condition.
rules.subject: Contracts

rules.section: Discharge of the Duty to Perform
core: (1) an unforeseeable event occurs (e.g., a natural disaster) making performance extremely difficult
(2) the nonoccurrence of the event was a basic assumption at the time of the contract
(3) the party seeking discharge was not at fault

(2) t n o t e w a b a a t t o t c
(3) t p s d w n a f

(3) the p___ s___ d___ was not at f___
rules.prose: A party's duty to perform is discharged by impracticability if an unforeseeable event occurs making the performance of the contract extremely difficult, the nonoccurrence of the event was a basic assumption at the time of the contract, and the party seeking discharge was not at fault.
rules.tip: Non-extraordinary increases in the cost of performance are not a sufficient basis for this defense. Discuss all relevant theories for discharging a party's duty to perform.

rules.subject: Contracts

rules.section: Discharge of the Duty to Perform
core: An unexpected event destroys the party's purpose for entering the contract → rescission, even if performance is still possible.
– the nonoccurrence of the event was a basic assumption at the time of the contract
– the party seeking discharge was not at fault

rules.prose: If an unexpected event arises that destroys the party's purpose for entering the contract, the party will be entitled to rescind the contract, even if the performance is still possible. The nonoccurrence of the event must have been a basic assumption at the time of the contract, and the party seeking discharge must not have been at fault.
rules.name: Krell v. Henry
rules.subject: Contracts

core: Any promise or description that is part of the basis of the bargain, unless merely the seller's opinion or commendation of the goods' value.
Disclaimer clauses that conflict with express warranties are ignored.

rules.prose: Any promise or description that is part of the basis of the bargain is an express warranty, unless it is merely the seller's opinion or commendation of the value of the goods. Disclaimer clauses that conflict with express warranties are ignored.
rules.subject: Contracts

core: Applies when the seller is a merchant.
Merchantable: goods fit for their ordinary purpose and pass without objection in the trade under the contract description.
Disclaimer: a clear written "as is" statement, or an oral statement using the term "merchantability."

rules.prose: The implied warranty of merchantability applies when the seller is a merchant. To be merchantable, goods must be fit for their ordinary purpose and pass without objection in the trade under the contract description. This warranty can be disclaimed through a clear written "as is" statement or an oral statement that uses the term "merchantability."
rules.subject: Contracts

rules.prompt: Implied warranty of fitness for a particular purpose
core: Implied whenever:
– the seller has reason to know (from any source) that the buyer has a particular use for the goods, and
– the buyer is relying on the seller's skill to select the goods
The seller need NOT be a merchant.
Disclaimer: general language ("as is") — but must be in writing and conspicuous.

rules.prose: A warranty that the goods are fit for a particular purpose is implied whenever the seller has reason to know, from any source and not just from the buyer, that the buyer has a particular use for the goods, and the buyer is relying upon the seller's skill to select the goods. The seller need not be a merchant. This warranty can be disclaimed by general language such as "as is," but the disclaimer must be in writing and conspicuous.
rules.subject: Contracts

core: If a duty to perform exists and has not been discharged, a party's non-performance is a breach.
– indicates intent not to perform before performance is due → anticipatory breach/repudiation
– fails to perform on the date of performance → breach

– f t p o t d o p → b

rules.prose: If a duty to perform does exist and has not been discharged, a party's non-performance is a breach. If a party indicates prior to the time of performance that it intends to breach, this is an anticipatory breach or repudiation; otherwise, when a party fails to perform on the date of performance, the party is in breach.
rules.subject: Contracts

The non-breaching party can:
(1) treat as a breach and sue immediately
(2) suspend performance and demand performance
(3) cancel the contract
(4) wait for the breach, then sue

rules.prose: Under the common law, an anticipatory repudiation occurs when the promisor party clearly and unequivocally indicates through words or acts that it will not perform. The non-breaching party can treat the repudiation as a breach of contract and sue immediately, suspend its own performance and demand performance from the promisor, cancel the contract, or wait for the breach to occur and then sue for breach.
rules.name: Hochster v. De La Tour
rules.subject: Contracts

rules.prose: Under the UCC, a buyer or seller repudiates by unequivocally refusing to perform or by failing to provide adequate assurances within a reasonable time, not to exceed 30 days, of the other party demanding them. Either party can demand assurances in writing if it has reasonable grounds to be insecure about the other party's ability to perform, and it may suspend performance until it receives assurances.
rules.subject: Contracts

core: – CL: the repudiating party can retract until the other party (i) acts in reliance, (ii) accepts the repudiation, or (iii) has filed a breach action
– UCC: can retract until the other party has cancelled the contract or materially changed position

rules.prose: At common law, the repudiating party can retract its repudiation until the other party acts in reliance on the repudiation, accepts the repudiation, or has already filed an action for breach of contract. Under the UCC, the repudiating party can retract until the other party has cancelled the contract or materially changed position on the basis of the repudiation.
rules.subject: Contracts

core: – Material: the non-breaching party does not receive the substantial benefit of the bargain → may withhold performance and pursue remedies
– Minor: the breaching party substantially but not fully performed → non-breaching party may pursue remedies but must still perform

rules.prose: A material breach occurs when the non-breaching party does not receive the substantial benefit of the bargain; the non-breaching party can withhold any promised performance and pursue remedies for breach. A minor breach occurs when the breaching party has substantially performed, but not fully performed; the non-breaching party is entitled to pursue remedies for the minor breach, but it still must perform under the contract.
rules.tip: If the facts are not clear on whether a material or minor breach occurred, discuss both rules and present the arguments the parties would make for both theories.
rules.subject: Contracts

rules.prompt: Perfect tender rule and nonconforming goods (UCC)
core: Perfect tender: the parties (including the seller) must strictly perform all duties under the contract, or they are in breach.
– The buyer has a right to inspect the goods; once he accepts them, he must pay
– Nonconforming tender or goods → the buyer may accept or reject all or part of the goods

rules.prose: Under the UCC perfect tender rule, the parties, including the seller, must strictly perform all duties under the contract or they will be in breach. The buyer has a right to inspect the goods, and once he accepts them, he has an obligation to pay. If either the tender or the goods are nonconforming, the buyer has the right to accept or reject all or part of the goods.

rules.subject: Contracts

rules.subject: Contracts

– must be foreseeable
– the amount must be proved with reasonable certainty
– generally based on the contract price for performance and the fair market value of performance

rules.prose: Expectation damages are intended to put the injured party in the same position as if the contract were performed. Expectation damages must be foreseeable, and the non-breaching party must be able to prove the amount of damages with reasonable certainty. Generally, the amount of damages will be based on the contract price for performance and the fair market value of performance.
rules.tip: Use the specific dollar amounts provided in the facts and show the calculations that lead to the ultimate amount of damages — the calculations are the same as analysis and earn points.
rules.name: Hawkins v. McGee
rules.subject: Contracts

core: Reasonably foreseeable damages beyond expectation damages, related to the breach (lost profit, lost business).
(1) Foreseeability: natural and probable consequences of the breach, or contemplated at contract formation
(2) Causation: caused by the defendant's breach
(3) Certainty: dollar amount proved with reasonable certainty (new business → too speculative; no award)

rules.prose: Consequential damages are reasonably foreseeable damages other than expectation damages that are related to the breach of the contract, such as loss of profit or lost business. To recover, the damages must be natural and probable consequences of the breach or contemplated by the parties at the time the contract was formed; the plaintiff must show a causal link between the damages and the defendant's breach; and the plaintiff must prove the dollar amount with reasonable certainty. When the amount of money is too speculative, such as for a new business, the court will not award consequential damages.
rules.name: Hadley v. Baxendale
rules.subject: Contracts

core: Damages the non-breaching party incurs in reasonable reliance upon the promise that the other party would perform.
A party CANNOT recover both reliance and expectation damages — it must choose between them.

rules.prose: Reliance damages are the damages that the non-breaching party incurs in reasonable reliance upon the promise that the other party would perform. A party cannot recover both reliance and expectation damages; the party must choose between them.
rules.tip: Although the party must ultimately choose, discuss both items of damages on the exam.
rules.subject: Contracts

rules.subject: Contracts

core: The non-breaching party has a duty to avoid or mitigate its damages, to the extent possible, by seeking replacements or substitutes.
– held to a standard of reasonable conduct
– a failure to mitigate reduces the damages recovered

rules.prose: The non-breaching party has a duty to avoid or mitigate its damages, to the extent possible, by seeking replacement or substitutes for the goods or services. The non-breaching party will be held to a standard of reasonable conduct, and a failure to mitigate damages will reduce the damages recovered by the non-breaching party.
rules.subject: Contracts

core: Restitution: recovery under an unjust enrichment theory (not based on the contract), available to non-breaching AND breaching parties.
– Typically: the non-breaching party partially performed, the other party breached → recovers the benefit conferred
– NOT available if the non-breaching party fully performed and only payment is due (seek expectation damages)
– Measure: reasonable cost to obtain the benefit from another source, or the increase in the breaching party's wealth

rules.prose: Restitution allows both non-breaching and breaching parties to recover under an unjust enrichment theory, not based on the contract. It usually arises when the non-breaching party has partially performed and the other party then breaches; the non-breaching party seeks restitution for the benefit conferred. A non-breaching party cannot seek restitution if it has performed all of its duties and only payment is due; it must seek expectation damages instead. The amount is measured by the reasonable cost for the breaching party to obtain the benefit from another source, or the increase in the breaching party's wealth from having received the benefit.
rules.subject: Contracts

core: A party that has not substantially performed is in breach and cannot recover under the contract.
But if the non-breaching party benefited, the breaching party recovers the benefit conferred MINUS the damages the non-breaching party is entitled to.

rules.prose: If a party has not substantially performed, it will be in breach of contract and cannot recover under the contract. However, if the non-breaching party has benefited from the breaching party's performance, the breaching party can recover for the benefit conferred minus the damages the non-breaching party is entitled to.
rules.subject: Contracts

core: Where there is no enforceable contract (or none at all), a court awards restitution damages if:
(1) the plaintiff conferred a measurable benefit on the defendant
(2) the plaintiff acted without gratuitous intent (intended to be paid)
(3) unfair to let the defendant retain the benefit: defendant could have declined but did not, or plaintiff had a reasonable excuse (e.g., emergency)

rules.prose: In certain situations where there is no enforceable contract, or a contract does not exist at all, a court will award restitution damages on the basis of quasi-contract if the plaintiff conferred a measurable benefit on the defendant, the plaintiff acted without gratuitous intent (intending to be paid), and it would be unfair to let the defendant retain the benefit because the defendant had an opportunity to decline the benefit but did not do so, or the plaintiff had a reasonable excuse for not giving the defendant that opportunity, such as an emergency.
rules.subject: Contracts

core: (1) valid contract (offer, acceptance, consideration)
(2) terms certain and clear enough for a court order
(3) conditions precedent satisfied (or excused) — performance due
(4) money damages inadequate — usually a unique item (land, art, antique cars)
(5) feasible to enforce and supervise (no service contracts)

rules.prose: The non-breaching party can ask a court to order the breaching party to perform the contract if there is a valid contract, the terms are certain and clear enough to allow the court to make an order, the non-breaching party has satisfied any conditions precedent (or the condition has been excused) so the breaching party's performance is due, money damages are inadequate — usually because the items involved are unique, such as land, pieces of art, or antique cars — and it is feasible for the court to enforce and supervise the performance. Courts will not require parties to perform service contracts.
rules.tip: Discuss all elements of the rule to earn full credit. Injunctive relief is often tested together with specific performance — the party can also seek an injunction ordering the seller not to sell the goods or land to a different, third-party buyer.
rules.subject: Contracts

core: – Laches: the non-breaching party waited an unreasonably long time to seek specific performance, and the delay prejudiced the breaching party
– Unclean hands: the non-breaching party itself engaged in unethical or immoral acts relating to the contract

rules.prose: A court may deny specific performance under laches if the non-breaching party waited an unreasonably long time to seek specific performance and the delay prejudiced the breaching party, or under unclean hands if the non-breaching party itself engaged in unethical or immoral acts relating to the contract.

rules.prompt: Third-party beneficiary — intended vs. incidental; vesting; who may sue
core: – The parties intend that performance by one party benefits a third party (not a party to the contract)
– Intended beneficiary: can sue; incidental beneficiary: CANNOT sue on the contract
– Modification OK until the 3PB's rights vest; otherwise 3PB notice and consent needed
– Donee 3PB (promise is a gift): generally may only sue the promisor (promisee too, if told of the contract + justifiable detrimental reliance)
– Creditor 3PB: may sue either promisor or promisee (only 1 recovery allowed)

rules.prose: A third-party beneficiary contract arises when the parties intend that performance by one party benefits a third party who is not a party to the contract. An intended beneficiary can sue; an incidental beneficiary cannot sue on the contract. The contract may be modified until the beneficiary's rights vest; after that, the beneficiary's notice and consent are needed. A donee beneficiary generally may only sue the promisor, but may sue the promisee if the promisee told the beneficiary about the contract and the beneficiary justifiably relied to his detriment. A creditor beneficiary may sue either the promisor or the promisee, but only one recovery is allowed.
rules.subject: Contracts

rules.prompt: Assignment of rights vs. delegation of duties
core: – Assignment: a right to a contract benefit is transferred to a third party after formation; no formalities needed
– improper if it materially increases a duty; assignee stands in the shoes of the assignor
– Delegation: a duty to perform the contract is transferred to a third party after formation
– generally OK unless it involves personal judgment or skill
– delegator still liable; delegatee cannot be compelled to perform unless he assumed the duty (promise + consideration)

rules.prose: An assignment transfers a right to a contract benefit to a third party after the contract is formed; no formalities are needed, but the assignment cannot materially increase a duty, and the assignee stands in the shoes of the assignor. A delegation transfers a duty to perform the contract to a third party after formation; it is generally proper unless it involves personal judgment or skill. The delegator remains liable, and the delegatee cannot be compelled to perform unless he has assumed the duty by a promise supported by consideration.

– intent to inflict great bodily injury
– reckless indifference to an unjustifiably high risk to human life (depraved-heart killing)

Malice aforethought will be found if the killing is committed with intent to kill, intent to inflict great bodily injury, reckless indifference to an unjustifiably high risk to human life, or under the felony-murder rule.
rules.tip: Address each way of finding malice in a separate paragraph to receive the maximum points.

rules.prompt: Felony-murder rule — statement and the dangerous felonies
core: FMR: D is guilty of an unintended and foreseeable killing proximately caused by or during the commission or attempted commission of an inherently dangerous felony.
Traditionally, the inherently dangerous felonies are burglary, arson, robbery, rape, and kidnapping.

rules.prose: Under the felony-murder rule, the defendant can be found guilty for an unintended and foreseeable killing that is proximately caused by or during the commission or attempted commission of an inherently dangerous felony. Traditionally, the inherently dangerous felonies are burglary, arson, robbery, rape, and kidnapping.
rules.tip: Only discuss felony murder when a separate inherently dangerous felony appears — the homicide itself does not trigger the FMR. Otherwise briefly state that the FMR is a way to find malice but no facts support it.

– Point of safety: felony complete and D has reached a place of safety → the FMR does not apply to a later killing

rules.prose: Under the majority rule, followed in California, the defendant is not liable for a third party's death caused by the victim or a police officer; the minority rule makes the defendant liable for any foreseeable third-party death. Under the Redline doctrine, the defendant is generally not guilty of felony murder when the victim or a police officer, acting in self-defense or trying to prevent escape, kills the cofelon. And if the killing occurs after the commission of the felony is complete and the defendant has reached a place of safety, the felony-murder rule will not apply.

– Premeditated: D had enough time to reflect on the idea of or plan the killing — even a mere second of reflection suffices
– Deliberate: D made the decision to kill in a cool and dispassionate manner

rules.prose: First-degree murder is a statutorily created category of murder that is premeditated and deliberate; oftentimes a jurisdiction will also characterize felony murder as a first-degree offense. A murder is premeditated if the defendant had enough time to reflect on the idea of or plan the killing, and even a mere second of reflection is sufficient. A murder is deliberate if the defendant made the decision to kill in a cool and dispassionate manner.
rules.tip: The felony-murder analysis for first-degree murder is the same as at common law — you can say "see above for the felony-murder analysis."

rules.prose: For voluntary manslaughter, there must be a provocation that would arouse a sudden and intense passion in the mind of an ordinary person, the defendant must have actually been provoked, there must not have been sufficient time for an ordinary person to cool off, and the defendant must not have actually cooled off. If the defendant's use of deadly force was inappropriate but the defendant believed such force was appropriate, imperfect self-defense might mitigate a murder charge down to voluntary manslaughter.
rules.tip: Easy to miss — if a murder question raises provocation and time-to-cool facts, bring up voluntary manslaughter even though the question has not asked for it.

rules.prose: If a third party's unlawful threat causes the defendant to reasonably believe that the only way to avoid death or serious bodily injury to himself or another is to violate the law, and it causes the defendant to do so, the defendant can claim duress; the fear of harm must be actual, subjective, and reasonable. Duress is not a defense to murder, but a defendant charged with felony murder can assert duress as a defense to the underlying felony and avoid conviction for felony murder.

rules.prose: Voluntary statements are not protected by Miranda and are admissible. Statements obtained in violation of a defendant's Miranda rights are excluded as substantive evidence, but are admissible for impeachment purposes if the defendant testifies. rules.prose: The Sixth Amendment right to counsel automatically applies at all critical stages of prosecution after formal proceedings begin. The right attaches when the state initiates prosecution through an indictment or a formal charge, and it ends at the sentencing stage.

rules.prose: A defendant has a right to be free of double jeopardy for the same offense under the Fifth Amendment, applicable to the states through the Fourteenth Amendment. He is protected against a second criminal prosecution for the same offense after acquittal or conviction, and against multiple punishments; the protection does not apply to civil actions. Jeopardy attaches once the jury is empaneled and sworn in, or, in a bench trial, after the first witness is sworn in. Crimes are the same offense unless each requires proof of an element that the other does not; offenses with different victims are separate, and separate sovereigns may each try the defendant.

rules.prose: A duty of care is owed to all foreseeable persons that may be injured by a defendant's failure to meet the reasonable standard of care. Under the majority view (Cardozo), a defendant is only liable to plaintiffs within the zone of foreseeable harm. Under the minority view (Andrews), a defendant owes a duty to everyone harmed.

Under the traditional approach, the standard of care depends upon the land entrant's status: an invitee is a public invitee or business visitor, such as a customer in a store; a licensee enters with the land possessor's permission or with a privilege, such as a social guest; and a trespasser lacks consent or privilege to be on the land.

rules.prose: A defendant engaged in an abnormally dangerous activity will be held strictly liable, without proof of negligence, for personal injuries and property damage caused by the activity. An abnormally dangerous activity is one with a high risk of harm that is not commonly found in the community and that has a risk that cannot be eliminated with due care.

– Assumption of the risk (voluntary + knowing) may bar recovery — not the only defense, but the best one

rules.prose: To be liable, the abnormally dangerous activity must be both the actual and proximate cause of the plaintiff's injury. To recover damages, the plaintiff must prove actual injury and not just economic loss. The plaintiff's recovery may be barred if she voluntarily and knowingly assumed the risk of her behavior; assumption of the risk is not the only defense, but it is the best defense.

rules.prose: A defendant who is in the business of selling a commercial product may be strictly liable for a defective product causing foreseeable injuries to a plaintiff. To establish a prima facie case, the plaintiff must prove that the defendant had an absolute duty as a commercial seller of the product, that the defendant produced or sold a defective product, actual and proximate causation, and damages.

rules.prose: Depending on the jurisdiction, courts usually apply either the consumer-expectation test or the risk-utility test (or a hybrid of both) to determine whether a design defect exists. Under the consumer-expectation test, the plaintiff must prove that the product is dangerous beyond the expectation of an ordinary consumer. Under the risk-utility test, the plaintiff must prove that a reasonable alternative design that is economically feasible was available to the defendant, and the failure to use that design rendered the product unreasonably unsafe.
rules.tip: Look for facts about the availability of alternative designs and the cost of these alternative designs.

rules.prompt: Strict products liability — causation and damages
core: – Actual cause: the product must have been defective when it LEFT defendant's control
– Proximate cause: the defect caused injury while the product was used in an intended or reasonably foreseeable way

– Damages: personal injury or property damage

rules.prose: To be the actual cause of the plaintiff's injury, the product must have been defective when it left the defendant's control. To be the proximate cause, the defect causing the plaintiff's injuries must have occurred when the product was being used in an intended or reasonably foreseeable way; a defendant will still be liable if the plaintiff misuses the product and is injured, if that misuse is foreseeable. The plaintiff must also suffer personal injury or property damage.
rules.subject: Torts

core: – Contributory-negligence jurisdiction: complete bar when plaintiff unreasonably proceeded in the face of a KNOWN product defect
– Pure comparative-negligence jurisdiction: damages reduced by the proportion of plaintiff's fault
– Assumption of the risk: voluntary + knowing → recovery may be barred

rules.prose: In a contributory-negligence jurisdiction, the plaintiff's negligence is a complete bar to recovery when the plaintiff's fault consisted of unreasonably proceeding in the face of a known product defect. In a pure comparative-negligence jurisdiction, the plaintiff's negligence is not a complete bar, but her damages are reduced by the proportion that her fault bears to the total harm. Recovery may also be barred if the plaintiff voluntarily and knowingly assumed the risk of her behavior.

rules.prose: General damages are presumed when the comments involve libel, which are written statements. However, they are not presumed when it involves slander, which are oral comments. rules.prose: If the plaintiff is either a public official or a public figure, he is required to prove that the defendant acted with actual malice, meaning the defendant either had knowledge that the statement was false or acted with reckless disregard as to the truth or falsity of the statement. If the plaintiff is a private individual and the defendant's statement involves a matter of public concern, the plaintiff is constitutionally required to prove that the defendant acted with fault — either negligence or actual malice.

rules.prompt: Transferred intent — three circumstances (and which torts)
core: Transferred intent exists in 3 circumstances:
(1) intends a tort against a person → commits a DIFFERENT tort against that person
(2) intends a tort against a person → commits the intended tort against a DIFFERENT person
(3) intends a tort against a person → commits a different tort against a different person
Applies to: battery, assault, false imprisonment, trespass to land, trespass to chattels.
NOT: IIED or conversion.

rules.prose: Transferred intent exists when a defendant intends to commit an intentional tort against a person but commits a different tort against that person, when a defendant intends to commit a tort against a person but commits the intended tort against a different person, and when a defendant intends to commit a tort against a person but commits a different tort against a different person. Transferred intent applies to battery, assault, false imprisonment, trespass to land, and trespass to chattels — not to IIED or conversion.

rules.prose: Supplemental jurisdiction applies when a plaintiff brings a state-law claim that relates to the plaintiff's preexisting federal question or diversity jurisdiction claim. rules.prose: Venue determines the proper forum among multiple courts having personal and subject matter jurisdiction. Venue is proper in any judicial district where any defendant resides if all defendants reside in the same state — an individual's residency is established where domiciled, while a business's residency requires a personal jurisdiction analysis for that particular district. Venue is also proper in a judicial district where a substantial part of the events or omissions occurred or the property is located. Contract disputes are resolved under the governmental-interest test, but if the contract has a choice-of-law provision, the provision is honored unless it is contrary to public policy. Tort claims are resolved under the governmental-interest test.

In California, substitute service requires a follow-up mailing to the defendant. Service on foreign individuals and corporations may be made by any method permitted by the law of the foreign country or by certified mail, but personal service on a foreign corporation is not allowed.

– Rule 9: heightened standards — circumstances constituting fraud or mistake stated with particularity
– California fact pleading: all material, operative facts constituting each cause of action + demand for relief + amount of damages

rules.prose: Under Rule 8, a complaint must include a short and plain statement of the claim sufficient to show that the pleader is entitled to relief, and must plead facts sufficient to show that the claim has substantive plausibility — though a party is not required to show probable entitlement to relief; a recitation of the elements and broad conclusory statements are insufficient. Under Rule 9, certain pleadings have heightened standards: a complaint must state with particularity the circumstances constituting fraud or mistake. California courts require fact pleading — all material, operative facts constituting each cause of action, a demand for relief, and the amount of damages.

– California: broader — all material relevant to the SUBJECT MATTER of the litigation; includes electronically stored information

rules.prose: Under the federal rules, a party may discover any nonprivileged matter relevant to a party's claim or defense and proportional to the case's needs; the court will consider whether the burden or expense of the proposed discovery outweighs its likely benefit. Information need not itself be admissible — it is discoverable if reasonably calculated to lead to the discovery of admissible evidence relevant to a claim or defense. California applies a broader standard, requiring disclosure of all material relevant to the subject matter of the litigation, and the scope of discovery includes electronically stored information as well.

Privileges include attorney-client, work product, and physician-patient, which does not exist under federal law but does under California law. Privilege is determined under federal common law for federal question cases and under state law for diversity or supplemental jurisdiction cases. California provides a right to privacy, which the court balances with the need for discovery.

core: Protects materials prepared by a party in anticipation of litigation. Discoverable only if:

Work product is discoverable only if the materials contain information not reasonably available by other means and the party seeking discovery would be substantially prejudiced if not allowed to access the materials. rules.prose: A party is entitled to depose any expert witness of an opposing party whose opinions may be presented at trial. Drafts of the expert witness report and communications between a party's attorney and an expert witness are protected, unless they relate to compensation or the facts, data, or assumptions the expert relied upon in forming his opinion. The report of an expert retained in anticipation of litigation but not expected to be called as a witness is discoverable only on a showing of exceptional circumstances. In California, all parties must disclose expert witness lists, details of prospective trial testimony, and all writings and reports.

Under the federal rules, depositions can take place anytime after the mandatory initial disclosures and are limited to 10 per party unless good cause is shown to the court. rules.prose: Under the federal rules, a party may serve no more than 25 interrogatories on any other party; in California, a party may serve unlimited Judicial Council form interrogatories and up to 35 specially drafted interrogatories. Interrogatories may not be served on nonparties and may relate to any nonprivileged matter relevant to any party's claim or defense, so long as proportional to the needs of the case. Each interrogatory must be answered fully and separately under oath unless the party objects; grounds for objection must be stated with specificity, and any ground not timely stated is waived unless the court excuses the failure for good cause.

rules.prose: If a party fails to make disclosures or respond to discovery, the other party may compel the party's responses. The parties must meet and confer in good faith to settle the discovery dispute — in California, a reasonable and good-faith effort to informally resolve the issues before filing. If the dispute is not resolved, the party may file a motion to compel discovery; if the motion is granted and the other party still fails to make disclosures or respond, the court may impose sanctions.

rules.prose: The federal right to a jury trial arises from the Seventh Amendment; in California, the right arises from the state constitution instead of the U.S. Constitution. Actions at law, such as actions for damages, generally may be tried on demand to a jury, while actions in equity, such as actions for an injunction, carry no right to a jury trial. When an action involves legal and equitable claims, the jury normally determines the legal claims first, and then the court determines the equitable claims.

rules.prose: Summary judgment is granted when there is no genuine issue of material fact and the moving party is entitled to judgment as a matter of law. The court will construe all evidence in the light most favorable to the nonmoving party, and the evidence considered includes discovery, such as depositions and interrogatories, and affidavits, so long as the facts are admissible at trial. The motion may be filed up until 30 days after the close of discovery; in California, 60 days after the general appearance, with papers served by 75 days before the hearing.

– Typicality — claims or defenses of the representative are typical of the class
– Common-question type: common questions must predominate + notice to individual members required
– (CA) an ascertainable class + a well-defined community of interest; claims aggregated for amount in controversy

rules.prose: You cannot have a class action without commonality — common questions of law or fact supporting the claim; adequacy — representatives who adequately and fairly protect the class; numerosity — a class so large that joinder is impracticable; and typicality — claims or defenses of the representative that are typical of the class. In the common-question type, common legal or factual questions must predominate and notice to the individual members is required. California uses different terminology: there must be an ascertainable class and a well-defined community of interest among the members, and claims are aggregated to meet the amount in controversy.

rules.prose: Under Proposition 8 of the California Constitution, any evidence that is relevant may be admitted in a criminal case. However, Prop. 8 makes an exception for balancing under California Evidence Code 352, which gives a court discretion to exclude relevant evidence if its probative value is substantially outweighed by a risk of unfair prejudice, confusion of issues, or misleading the jury.

Once the defendant opens the door, the prosecution may call a witness to rebut the defendant's claim of good character with reputation or opinion evidence, or cross-examine the defendant's character witness with reputation, opinion, or specific bad acts.
