– Client holds; only the client (or, e.g., a guardian) can waive
– Lasts indefinitely (federal); ends when the client dies (CA)
– Attorney work product is protected
– Exceptions: services sought for future crime/fraud, or client in dispute with attorney
– (CA) also if the lawyer reasonably believes disclosure necessary to prevent death/substantial bodily harm

rules.prose: The attorney-client privilege protects communications made during or when seeking professional legal services. The client holds the privilege, and only the client (or, for example, a guardian) can waive it; it lasts indefinitely under federal law but ends when the client dies in California. The attorney's work product is also protected. The privilege does not apply if the services were sought for a future crime or fraud or if the client is in a dispute with the attorney, or, in California, if the lawyer reasonably believes disclosure is necessary to prevent death or substantial bodily harm.
rules.subject: Evidence

– Patient is the holder of both
– Exceptions: patient's physical/mental condition at issue; statement made as part of crime or tort; physician-patient dispute
– (CA) psychotherapist exception if the patient is a danger and disclosure is needed to prevent the danger

rules.prose: The physician-patient privilege protects statements made for the purposes of obtaining medical treatment — in California, treatment or diagnosis, and the privilege is limited to civil cases. The psychotherapist-patient privilege protects statements made to a psychotherapist or licensed social worker. The patient is the holder of both privileges. Exceptions apply when the patient's physical or mental condition is at issue, when the statement was made as part of a crime or tort, or when a dispute exists between physician and patient; in California, also when the patient is a danger and disclosure is needed to prevent the danger.
rules.subject: Evidence

rules.prompt: Expert testimony — admissibility requirements (FRE Daubert vs. CA Frye)
core: Daubert — allowed if scientific, technical, or specialized testimony will help the trier of fact:
– qualified by knowledge, skill, training, experience, or education
– based on sufficient facts or data personally observed or made aware of
– product of reliable methods reasonably relied on by experts in the field, applied reliably to the facts
– reasonably certain in her opinion (probably true, no guessing)
– may embrace ultimate issue but NOT criminal defendant's mental state
(CA) Frye — requires a preliminary showing the science is generally accepted and reliable

rules.prose: Under Daubert, expert testimony is allowed if scientific, technical, or specialized testimony will help the trier of fact. The expert must be qualified by knowledge, skill, training, experience, or education; the opinion must be based on sufficient facts or data personally observed or made aware of, be the product of reliable methods reasonably relied on by experts in the field, applied reliably to the facts, and be reasonably certain — probably true, no guessing. The opinion may embrace the ultimate issue but not a criminal defendant's mental state. In California, Frye requires a preliminary showing that the science is generally accepted and reliable.

rules.prose: Under the Sixth Amendment Confrontation Clause, to admit a testimonial hearsay statement against a criminal defendant, he must have had an opportunity to confront and cross-examine the declarant. Whether a statement is testimonial turns on its primary purpose; statements to police during an emergency are not testimonial. For the forfeiture-by-wrongdoing exception to apply, the defendant must have acted with the purpose to make the witness unavailable to testify. Face-to-face confrontation between a defendant and witness is favored but not absolute; an exception is allowed for the public interest, such as a child accuser testifying remotely.

Even if harm will result, may withdraw if:
– client engages in criminal or fraudulent action
– client used the lawyer's services to commit crime or fraud
– client insists on a repugnant course or one the lawyer fundamentally disagrees with
– client made representation unreasonably difficult (includes failure to communicate)
– unreasonable financial burden on the lawyer (NOT a basis in CA)

rules.prose: Under the Model Rules, a lawyer may seek to withdraw at any time if he can do so without materially harming the client. Even if harm will result, a lawyer can withdraw if the client engages in criminal or fraudulent action, the attorney learns the client used his services to commit criminal or fraudulent action, the client insists on a course of action the lawyer finds repugnant or fundamentally disagrees with, the client has made representation unreasonably difficult, or the representation will result in an unreasonable financial burden on the lawyer.

– claim or defense not warranted under existing law + no good-faith argument for change
– client pursues, or used the lawyer's services to advance, a crime or fraud
– client insists the lawyer pursue criminal or fraudulent conduct
– client's conduct renders effective representation unreasonably difficult
– continued representation likely to violate the CA rules or State Bar Act

rules.prose: Under the California rules, a lawyer may not withdraw merely because it can be done without material harm to the client. Instead, a lawyer may seek to withdraw when the client insists on a claim or defense not warranted under existing law and unsupportable by a good-faith argument for changing the law; the client pursues, insists the lawyer pursue, or has used the lawyer's services to advance a criminal or fraudulent course of conduct; the client renders it unreasonably difficult for the lawyer to carry out the representation effectively; or continued representation is likely to violate the California rules or the State Bar Act.

A lawyer who wants to withdraw when litigation is pending must obtain court approval, and a court may order the attorney to continue representing the client if withdrawal would materially harm the client's case.

rules.prose: Upon termination, a lawyer has a duty to minimize the harm to the client. The lawyer must return all papers and property to the client, and must safeguard the client's property and papers from damage while they are in the attorney's possession. Under the Model Rules, an attorney may retain papers in order to obtain payment in limited circumstances; the California rules require the return of all papers and property. An attorney must also promptly return any advanced fees that were not earned.

rules.prose: Lawyers in the same firm may split fees. Under the Model Rules, lawyers from different firms may split a fee if the fee is in proportion to the services provided by each lawyer, the client agrees in writing to the fee splitting, and the total fee is reasonable. Under the California rules, fee splitting is allowed if the lawyers enter into a written agreement to divide the fee, the client provides written consent after full disclosure of the fee splitting, and the total fee is not unconscionable. Under both, the total fees must not be higher because multiple attorneys are working on the case.
rules.tip: Commonly tested — analyze all elements of the rules, especially the written consent requirement.

rules.prose: A lawyer is prohibited from disclosing any information relating to the representation of a client, unless the disclosure is authorized by the informed consent of the client or impliedly authorized in order to carry out the representation. rules.prose: Under the California rules, a lawyer is prohibited from making an agreement prospectively limiting malpractice liability to a client. Under the Model Rules, in contrast, this prohibition does not apply if the client is represented by another independent lawyer in making the agreement.
rules.subject: Professional Responsibility

– not both sides of the same litigation or proceeding

rules.prose: A lawyer representing an organization owes duties of loyalty and confidentiality to the organization, not its individual constituents, such as officers, directors, and employees. A lawyer may represent an organization and its constituents as long as there is no conflict. When speaking with the organization's constituents, the attorney must make it clear who the attorney is representing when it is or becomes apparent that the organization's interests are or may become adverse to the constituent.

rules.prose: Under the Model Rules, a lawyer is obligated to provide competent representation to a client and must possess the legal knowledge, skill, thoroughness, and preparation reasonably necessary for the representation. Under both rules, a lawyer may accept representation if competence can be achieved by reasonable preparation or association with another lawyer who has competence in the field.

Under the California rules, a lawyer must not bring or continue an action, conduct a defense, assert a position in litigation, or take an appeal without probable cause and for the purpose of harassing or maliciously injuring any person.

rules.prose: If a lawyer learns about false evidence after it has been presented, the lawyer must take reasonable remedial measures, and must urge the client to correct the false statement or evidence. Under the Model Rules, if withdrawal is not permitted, the lawyer must disclose the false evidence to the court, even if that requires revealing otherwise protected confidential information. Under the California rules, the lawyer may not disclose confidential information that is protected by the rules. rules.prose: An attorney can refuse to offer a criminal defendant's testimony only if he knows it is false; if he reasonably believes but does not know it is false, he must offer it. If he knows the testimony is or will be false, he must urge the client to correct it. If the client refuses, the attorney must withdraw under the Model Rules; under the California rules, withdrawal is optional. If withdrawal is denied, in California the lawyer can question the defendant until the point of the falsehood and then let him testify in narrative form; under the Model Rules, the lawyer must disclose the false evidence to the court.

rules.prose: A lawyer is not permitted to make an out-of-court statement that he knows or should know will be distributed publicly and will have a substantial likelihood of materially prejudicing the court proceeding. General facts about the case, such as the claims and the identity of the parties, may be discussed publicly, and a lawyer may make a statement to protect a client from the prejudicial effect of publicity that was not initiated by the attorney or the client. In criminal trials, out-of-court statements are more likely to be prejudicial.

rules.prose: A prosecutor must not prosecute a charge that he knows is not supported by probable cause. A prosecutor is required to timely disclose all exculpatory evidence and information that tends to disprove guilt or mitigates the offense. The prosecutor must also disclose any new, credible evidence creating a likelihood that a convicted defendant did not commit the offense.

rules.prose: A lawyer must not make false or materially misleading statements about himself or his services. Advertising must include the name and address of the attorney; attorneys can state that they specialize in an area of law, but a lawyer may not state or imply that she is a certified specialist in an area of law unless she is actually certified.

– a close personal friend, or

rules.prose: Solicitations are narrower, targeted communications directed at groups identified as requiring a specific service, such as car accident victims. Solicitation of prospective clients in person, by live telephone, or by real-time electronic contact is prohibited when a significant motive for the lawyer's action is the lawyer's pecuniary gain, unless the person contacted is a lawyer, a family member, a close personal friend, or someone with a prior professional relationship with the soliciting lawyer.

rules.section: Advertising, Solicitation, and Integrity of the Profession

rules.prose: Under the Model Rules, a lawyer is required to report misconduct by another attorney when the lawyer has actual knowledge of the misconduct. The duty is limited to misconduct relating to the attorney's honesty or professional fitness. The California rules now require a lawyer to report misconduct of another lawyer when the lawyer knows of credible evidence that another lawyer has engaged in misconduct that raises a substantial question as to that lawyer's honesty, trustworthiness, or fitness as a lawyer; actual knowledge of the misconduct is not required.

– MR: a disqualified lawyer may be screened off if given no part of the fee
– + the client gets written notice and certification of screening-compliance procedures at reasonable intervals on request
– CA: more restrictive — screening only in limited circumstances (lawyer did NOT substantially participate in the same or a substantially related matter at the old firm)

rules.prose: When one lawyer is disqualified, the disqualification extends to the rest of the firm, unless waived by the client or based on the lawyer's personal interest and without risk of affecting the representation; a conflicted nonlawyer (e.g., a paralegal) can be screened off. Under the Model Rules, a disqualified lawyer may be screened off if given no part of the fee and the client is given written notice and certification of screening compliance procedures at reasonable intervals upon request. California is more restrictive, permitting screening only in limited circumstances — where the lawyer did not substantially participate in the same or a substantially related matter at the old firm.

rules.prompt: Duties of supervising and subordinate lawyers
core: – Managerial lawyer: reasonable efforts to ensure the firm's lawyers AND nonlawyers comply with the rules — via reasonable policies and procedures
– Direct supervisory authority: reasonable efforts to ensure the supervised lawyer or nonlawyer complies with the ethical rules
– Knows of misconduct while its consequences can be avoided or mitigated → must take reasonable remedial action
– A subordinate lawyer is bound by the rules notwithstanding a supervisor's directive
– BUT not disciplined for obeying a REASONABLE resolution of an ARGUABLE question of professional duty

rules.prose: A lawyer with managerial duties must make reasonable efforts to ensure the firm's lawyers and nonlawyers comply with the rules of professional conduct by instituting reasonable policies and procedures. A lawyer with direct supervisory authority over another lawyer or nonlawyer must make reasonable efforts to ensure compliance with the applicable ethical rules, and if he knows of misconduct when its consequences can be avoided or mitigated, he must take reasonable remedial action. A subordinate lawyer is bound by the rules notwithstanding a directive from a supervising attorney, but will not be subject to discipline for obeying a reasonable resolution of an arguable question of professional duty.
rules.tip: Whenever more than one lawyer is named in the fact pattern, look for a supervisor/subordinate relationship to spot a breach of the duty to supervise — even if the supervising lawyer is not active in or does not know of the subordinate's misconduct. When the facts include a nonlawyer staff member, consider the policies and procedures in place and whether the partner made reasonable efforts to ensure staff compliance.

rules.prose: A grantor can place a reasonable restraint on the grantee's ability to freely transfer. A restraint is reasonable if it lasts only for a specified period of time, and unreasonable if the restriction continues indefinitely, applies to the grantee's heirs, or applies in perpetuity. Unreasonable restraints will be stricken from the conveyance.

rules.prose: A cotenant does not have a right to be reimbursed by other cotenants for repairs, even if necessary. However, the majority rule is that contribution for necessary repairs can be compelled in accounting or partition actions, and in some jurisdictions an action for contribution can be brought only if notice was given. When a third party is occupying the property, a cotenant who collects rent from the third party can subtract expenses for necessary repairs from the rent received before sharing the rent with the other cotenants.

core: – Tenancy for years: express agreement, fixed period of time
– Periodic tenancy: set periods that automatically renew (month to month)
– Tenancy at will: express agreement, no fixed period; either party may terminate anytime
– Tenancy at sufferance: tenant wrongfully holds over; lasts until eviction or converts to periodic

– T a w: e a, n f p; e p m t a

rules.prose: A tenancy for years is created by express agreement for a fixed period of time. A periodic tenancy is a repetitive, ongoing estate measured by set periods that automatically renew, such as month to month. A tenancy at will requires the parties' express agreement, has no fixed period of time, and may be terminated by either party at any time. A tenancy at sufferance arises when a tenant wrongfully holds over past the expiration of the lease, and lasts until eviction or converts into a periodic tenancy.

rules.prose: The implied warranty of habitability applies only to residential leases. The landlord must maintain the property such that it is reasonably suited for residence, and failing to comply with housing codes is a breach, especially violations related to health or safety. The tenant must give notice to the landlord and a reasonable opportunity to repair.

rules.prose: Constructive eviction occurs when the landlord substantially interferes with the tenant's use and enjoyment of the land; the tenant gives notice of the problem and a reasonable time for the landlord to repair, but the landlord does not repair; and the tenant vacates the premises in a reasonable amount of time.

rules.prose: If the lease prohibits only assignment, the tenant may still sublease. If the tenant violates the prohibition, the landlord can terminate the lease. rules.prose: Adverse possession requires possession that is continuous, actual, open and notorious, hostile, and exclusive. Seasonal or infrequent use may be sufficiently continuous if it is consistent with the type of property being possessed. rules.prose: Under a notice statute, if a person purchases land without notice of a prior interest, the person will prevail in an ownership dispute against the prior interest. Under a race statute, the first person to record their deed will prevail, regardless of knowledge. rules.tip: Recording acts also apply to mortgages for priority purposes.

rules.prompt: Covenant running with the land — six requirements
core: – Writing (Statute of Frauds)
– Intent: to bind future owners
– Touch and concern: affects the parties as owners of the land, not merely personally
– Notice (burden only): purchaser without notice (recording act) is not bound
– Horizontal privity (burden only): original parties shared an interest besides the promise (grantor-grantee)
– Vertical privity: burden needs the ENTIRE interest transferred; benefit runs with any portion

rules.prose: For a covenant to run with the land, there must be a writing complying with the Statute of Frauds; intent for the rights and duties to bind future owners; and touch and concern, meaning the benefit or burden affects the promisee and promisor as owners of land and not merely personally. For the burden to run, there must also be notice and horizontal privity — the original parties shared some interest other than the promise. Vertical privity requires the owner to transfer the entire interest for the burden to run; the benefit runs if the successor takes any portion of the original estate.
rules.tip: The facts usually involve one owner selling to a new party — determine whether the benefit or the burden runs. For intent, look for explicit language ("and his heirs and assigns") or implication from the totality of the document and circumstances.

rules.prose: Under the Rule Against Perpetuities, no interest in property is valid unless it must vest, if at all, within 21 years after some life in being at the creation of the interest. It applies to vested remainders subject to open, contingent remainders, executory interests, and powers of appointment. In a majority of jurisdictions, courts will "wait and see" if the interest actually vests within 90 years after its creation.

The surviving spouse of an intestate decedent is also entitled to the decedent's one-half of the community property and quasi-community property, so the surviving spouse will take one hundred percent of the community property.

– none → the spouse takes 100% of the decedent's SP
– one lineal descendant, or a parent or issue of a parent → spouse takes 1/2; the other surviving relative takes the other 1/2
– more than one lineal descendant → spouse takes 1/3; the other surviving relatives split the other 2/3 equally

rules.prose: If there are no surviving lineal descendants, parents, or issue of parents of the decedent, the surviving spouse gets all of the decedent's separate property. If the decedent is survived by one lineal descendant or by a parent or issue of a parent, the surviving spouse gets one-half, and the other surviving relative gets the other one-half. If the decedent is survived by more than one lineal descendant, the surviving spouse gets one-third, and the other surviving relatives split the other two-thirds equally.

rules.prose: If the surviving issue are all of equal degree of kinship, the property passes equally to each person. If the surviving issue are not of equal kinship, the property is divided at the first generation in which at least one member survives the decedent, and the shares that would go to a member who predeceased the decedent go to his or her issue.

If the testator does not sign in the presence of the witnesses, he must expressly or impliedly acknowledge his signature to them before they sign. rules.prose: A witness who has a financial interest in the will is an interested witness. In California, a will can be valid despite the presence of an interested witness, but a rebuttable presumption is created that the interested witness exerted undue influence over the testator. If the presumption is not rebutted, the witness takes his or her intestate share and will not take under the terms of the will.

A valid codicil executed after the original will cures any problems that existed at the execution of the will, including an interested witness; the interested witness will no longer be considered an interested witness and will take under the terms of the new codicil.

– Inconsistencies: the later document controls and revokes the prior inconsistencies

rules.prose: A testator can revoke a will or codicil by executing a later will or codicil that partly or completely revokes the prior will or codicil; oral revocations are not valid. The revocation can be express, where the new instrument expressly states that it is revoking the prior one, or implied, where the terms of the new instrument conflict with the prior one. If there are inconsistencies between the instruments, the later document controls and revokes the prior inconsistencies.
rules.tip: Carefully analyze whether the original will or codicil is partially or completely revoked, considering both sides; for implied revocation, explain why the new instrument conflicts with the prior will.

core: A court first classifies each gift:
– Specific: an item distinguishable with reasonable accuracy from other property of the estate (a specific car)
– General: meant to be satisfied from the general assets of the estate (money)
– Demonstrative: from a named source, then general funds if insufficient ($100,000 from my Bank of Columbia account)
– Residuary: the estate remaining after all claims and all specific, general, and demonstrative gifts are satisfied

rules.prose: A specific gift is an item that can be distinguished with reasonable accuracy from other property that is part of the testator's estate. A general gift is meant to be satisfied from the general assets of the estate. A demonstrative gift is paid from a named source, and from general funds if that source is insufficient. A residual gift is a gift of the estate remaining when all claims against the estate and all specific, general, and demonstrative gifts have been satisfied.

rules.prose: Traditionally, if a specific gift is missing, destroyed, or there is a substantial change in its form, the beneficiary takes nothing; this does not apply to general or demonstrative gifts. Under modern law and in California, courts will examine the testator's intent at the time he disposed of the specific gift. If there is evidence that the testator intended for the beneficiary to receive the gift, the court may treat the specific gift as a general or demonstrative gift or trace the specific gift to its new form.

– Class gift: a class member predeceases T → the surviving class members take, unless anti-lapse saves the share for the deceased member's issue

rules.prose: Under common law, if a beneficiary dies before the testator, the gift to the beneficiary lapses. Under the anti-lapse statute, applicable in California, the gift is saved if the predeceasing beneficiary is a relative of the testator or the testator's spouse who leaves issue; the issue will take the predeceased beneficiary's share. If there is a gift to a class and a class member predeceases the testator, the surviving class members will take unless the anti-lapse statute applies to save the share for the issue of the deceased class member.

rules.prose: A will not found at the testator's death will create a presumption that it was revoked by physical act. The burden is on the proponent to establish the existence of the will by clear and convincing evidence; duplicate originals may be used, but duplicate copies will not be admitted. core: – Per capita with representation (CA): divide equally at the FIRST generation with a living member; deceased members' shares pass to their surviving issue
– Per stirpes: divide into the total number of children who survive or leave surviving issue; the issue take their deceased ancestor's share in equal portions
– Per capita at each generation (UPC): divide at the first generation with a living member; COMBINE deceased members' shares and divide equally at the next generation

rules.prose: Under per capita with representation, followed in California, divide the property equally at the first generation with a living member; the shares of those who did not survive go to their surviving issue. Under per stirpes, divide the property into the total number of children who survive or leave issue who survive; the issue take in equal portions the share their deceased ancestor would have taken if living. Under per capita at each generation, the UPC method, divide at the first generation with a living member, then combine the shares of deceased members with surviving issue and divide equally at the next generation.
rules.tip: Know all three methods — a testator could elect any method via her will.

rules.section: General Principles and Status of the Parties
rules.prompt: General principles of community-property law — the opening paragraph
core: California is a community-property state.
– Marital economic community: begins upon marriage; ends at divorce, death of a spouse, or a permanent separation
– Property, earnings, or debt acquired during marriage: presumed community property
– Acquired before marriage, by gift or inheritance during marriage, or after divorce/permanent separation: presumed separate property
– Quasi-community property: acquired while living in a non-community-property state, but would be community property had the couple been living in CA at acquisition

rules.prose: California is a community-property state. In a community-property state, the marital economic community begins upon marriage and ends at divorce, death of a spouse, or a permanent separation. Property, earnings, or debt acquired during marriage are presumed to be community property. Property acquired by either spouse before marriage, by gift or inheritance during marriage, or after divorce or a permanent separation is presumed to be separate property. Finally, property acquired by a married couple while living in a non-community-property state that would be characterized as community property if the couple had been living in CA at the time of acquisition is called quasi-community property.
rules.tip: Always START the essay with this introductory paragraph. Add the quasi-community-property lines only where quasi-community property is relevant; do not cite those rules unless you need them.

(1) at least one spouse has expressed the intent to end the marriage, and
(2) the spouse's conduct is consistent with the intent to end the marriage

rules.prose: As of January 1, 2017, living separate and apart — physical separation — is no longer required for a permanent separation. Separation occurs when the parties have a complete and final break in the marital relationship, which must be proved by evidence that at least one spouse has expressed the intent to end the marriage and that the spouse's conduct is consistent with the intent to end the marriage. This law applies retroactively.
rules.tip: Past essays present couples not officially divorced but permanently separated with no intent to resume the marriage — the marital community has ended. Watch for ambiguous facts and analyze WHEN the community ended.

– no express contract: the court looks for an implied contract or understanding

rules.prose: An unmarried couple's property will be distributed based on contract principles. Express contracts setting forth the distribution of property will be enforced unless they are based upon meretricious sexual services. If there is no express contract, a court will look for an implied contract or understanding. A court may also distribute property based on unjust enrichment and fairness principles.
rules.tip: If an unmarried couple agrees about their property and later marries: property acquired before the marriage is distributed under the agreement; property acquired during the marriage is governed by community-property principles.

– may rely on community-property principles; entitled to a share of the community property upon death or divorce
– rights STOP accruing when the spouse discovers the marriage is not valid

rules.prose: A putative marriage occurs when one or both spouses have a good-faith belief that there is a valid marriage but it is not a valid marriage; the spouse who has a good-faith belief in the validity of the marriage is a putative spouse. A putative spouse may rely on community-property principles and is entitled to a share of the community property upon death or divorce. However, the putative spouse's rights stop accruing when he or she discovers the marriage is not valid.

rules.prose: The anti-Lucas legislation applies to property acquired after January 1, 1984. As of 1987, all jointly held property — joint tenancy, tenancy in common, or tenancy by the entirety — acquired during marriage is presumed to be community property upon divorce. This presumption can be rebutted by an express writing evidencing the spouses' intent to hold the property as separate property. If a spouse contributes separate property to the purchase of the property, she or he has a right to reimbursement for the amount of the contribution, but not any increase in value.

rules.prose: Before 1984, property taken in joint form — joint tenancy, tenancy in common, or tenancy by the entirety — was presumed to be community property upon divorce. This presumption could be rebutted by evidence that the spouses did not intend to hold the property as community property. Any separate property used to purchase the property was presumed to be a gift, and there was no right to reimbursement unless there was an agreement.

rules.prose: A transmutation refers to changing the nature of property from separate property to community property, community property to separate property, or the separate property of one spouse to the separate property of the other spouse. Under current law, to be valid, a transmutation must be in writing and contain language expressly stating that ownership of the property is being changed, by the spouse whose interest is adversely affected. Prior to January 1, 1985, oral agreements were sufficient to transmute property as long as there was evidence to support the transmutation.

rules.prose: Each spouse owes the other a duty to act in the highest of good faith with respect to the other spouse in the management and control of the community property. Failure of a spouse to obtain the consent of the other spouse when making gifts or selling or leasing property gives rise to a breach of duty. rules.prompt: Pereira approach — when used and formula
core: Used if the increase in value is attributable to the personal skills and effort (community property) of the managing spouse.
– Separate property = value of the business at marriage + (value at marriage × fair rate of return [use 10% on the exam] × years of marriage)
– Community property = fair market value of the business at divorce − the separate-property value

rules.prose: The Pereira approach is used if the increase in value can be attributed to the personal skills and effort of the managing spouse, which are community property. Separate property equals the value of the separate-property business at the time of marriage, plus the value at the time of marriage multiplied by a fair rate of return — use 10% on the exam — multiplied by the years of marriage. Community property equals the fair market value of the business at divorce minus the separate-property value as calculated.
rules.subject: Community Property

rules.prompt: Van Camp approach — when used and formula
core: Used when the primary reason for the increase in value is the character of the separate property itself, rather than the spouse's labor.
– Community property = (reasonable value of services − annual family expenses) × years of marriage
– Separate property = fair market value of the business at divorce − the community-property value

rules.prose: The Van Camp approach is used when the primary reason for the increase in value is a character of the separate property itself, rather than the labor of the spouse. Community property equals the reasonable value of the spouse's services minus annual family expenses, multiplied by the years of marriage. Separate property equals the fair market value of the business at divorce minus the community-property value as calculated.

rules.prose: When a spouse uses her separate property to improve the other spouse's separate property, she generally has a statutory right to be reimbursed for the contribution. A spouse using her own separate property to improve community property is entitled to reimbursement for the funds expended. When a spouse uses community property to improve the other spouse's separate property, the community is entitled to either reimbursement for the funds expended or the enhanced value of the property, whichever is greater.

If the stock options replace earnings after divorce or separation, then the options are separate property.

rules.prose: If a spouse dies without a will, the surviving spouse is entitled to one-half of the community property as well as the other one-half that the deceased spouse owned — 100% of the community property. The surviving spouse is entitled to all of the decedent's separate property if there are no surviving lineal descendants, parents, or issue of parents; one-half if the decedent is survived by one lineal descendant or by a parent or issue of a parent; and one-third if the decedent is survived by more than one lineal descendant.

Liability does not extend to debts incurred after permanent separation or divorce. The nondebtor spouse's community-property earnings can be protected by depositing them in a separate bank account.

rules.prose: Upon divorce, debts incurred before marriage will be assigned to the spouse who incurred the debt. Debts incurred during the marriage that were not for the benefit of the community will be assigned as separate property to the debtor spouse.

rules.prose: Necessaries of life are living costs, including food, clothing, shelter, and medical expenses. While living together and married, a spouse's community property and separate property are liable for the other spouse's debts relating to necessaries of life; upon divorce, the nondebtor spouse is no longer liable. While living apart, a spouse's community property and separate property are still liable for the other spouse's debts relating to expenses that are required to sustain life.
rules.tip: Example tested: during separation, one spouse incurred a hospital bill — an essential expense, so the hospital could recover from that spouse's separate property, the community property, and the other spouse's separate property.

rules.prose: A child- or spousal-support obligation from a previous relationship shall be treated as a debt incurred before marriage. The community property will be liable for the debt unless the nondebtor spouse places her earnings in a separate bank account that is not accessible to the debtor spouse. If the debtor spouse had separate property to pay the obligation but used community property, the nondebtor spouse can seek reimbursement to the community property.

rules.prompt: Piercing the corporate veil — rule and factors
core: Shareholders generally not personally liable — but a plaintiff may pierce the veil and sue shareholders individually (most likely closely held).
Totality of the circumstances, including:
– undercapitalization at formation
– disregard of corporate formalities (no annual meetings or votes)
– corporate assets used as the shareholder's own
– self-dealing with the corporation
– siphoning corporate funds or stripping assets

rules.prose: Generally, shareholders are not personally liable for corporate acts; however, courts may allow a plaintiff to pierce the corporate veil and sue a shareholder individually. To determine whether to pierce the corporate veil, the court will look at the totality of the circumstances, including undercapitalization of the corporation at the time of formation, disregard of corporate formalities, use of corporate assets as a shareholder's own assets, self-dealing with the corporation, and siphoning corporate funds or stripping assets.
rules.tip: Triggered by a third-party plaintiff seeking to recover money from the corporation. If shareholders used the corporation as a liability shield, ignored formalities, or treated it like a personal bank account, a court is likely to pierce.

Self-dealing and usurping corporate opportunities are violations.

rules.prose: The duty of loyalty requires a director to act in a manner that the director reasonably believes is in the best interest of the corporation. Self-dealing and usurping corporate opportunities are violations of the duty of loyalty.
rules.subject: Corporations

core: A transaction with the corporation that benefits:
– the director himself, or a closely related family member, or
– another corporation or partnership that the director (or a close family member) is associated with
Violates the duty of loyalty unless protected by the safe-harbor rule; the business judgment rule does NOT apply to self-dealing.

rules.prose: A director that engages in a transaction with a corporation that benefits himself, or a closely related family member, will be considered to have engaged in self-dealing, as will a transaction benefiting another corporation or partnership that the director or his closely related family member is associated with. Self-dealing violates the duty of loyalty unless the transaction is protected under the safe-harbor rule, and the business judgment rule does not apply to a self-dealing transaction.
rules.subject: Corporations

core: – the interested director discloses all material facts to the board AND a majority of disinterested directors approve
– the interested director discloses all material facts to the shareholders AND a majority of disinterested shareholder votes approve, or
– the transaction is fair to the corporation at the time of the deal — burden on the interested director to show substantive fairness

rules.prose: A self-dealing transaction can avoid violating the duty of loyalty in three ways: the interested director discloses all material facts to the board of directors and receives approval by a majority of disinterested directors; the interested director discloses all material facts to shareholders and receives approval by a majority of disinterested shareholder votes; or the transaction is fair to the corporation at the time of the deal, with the burden on the interested director to show that the substance of the transaction was fair.
rules.subject: Corporations

core: An unprotected self-dealing transaction can be enjoined or rescinded, and the corporation can seek damages from the interested director.

rules.subject: Corporations

core: A director violates the duty of loyalty by taking a corporate opportunity rather than offering it to the corporation first.
– Test: corporation seeking the opportunity, or within its current or prospective line of business?
– If so → present to the corporation first; take only after the corporation declines

rules.prose: A director may violate the duty of loyalty by usurping a corporate opportunity for business and taking the opportunity for himself rather than offering it to the corporation first. To determine whether a business opportunity must first be offered to the corporation, a court will ask if the corporation is seeking the opportunity or if the opportunity is within the corporation's current or prospective line of business. If it is a corporate opportunity, the director must present it to the corporation first, and only if the corporation declines may the director take the opportunity without violating the duty of loyalty.
rules.name: Guth v. Loft
rules.subject: Corporations

core: Typical officers: president, secretary, treasurer — elected by the board of directors.
An officer's authority is governed by agency law: the corporation is the principal, the officer is the agent; authority can be actual (express or implied) or apparent.

rules.prose: A typical corporation's officers are president, secretary, and treasurer, and they are elected by the board of directors. An officer's authority to act on behalf of a corporation is governed by agency law: the corporation is the principal and the officer is the agent, and authority can be actual, whether express or implied, or apparent.
rules.subject: Corporations

core: – Express actual: defined by the bylaws or set by the board of directors
– Implied actual: tasks necessary to carry out the duties of his position — but NO authority to bind the corporation to extraordinary acts
– Apparent: the corporation holds the officer out as having authority to bind it to third parties (e.g., the officer's position title)

rules.prose: Express authority is defined by the bylaws or set by the board of directors. An officer has implied authority to perform tasks necessary to carry out the duties of his position, but does not have authority to bind the corporation to extraordinary acts. An officer has apparent authority if the corporation holds the officer out as having authority to bind the corporation to third parties, as the officer's position title may do.
rules.subject: Corporations

core: Dissolution: a corporation may voluntarily terminate its status.
Winding up: a dissolved corporation may continue to exist for the limited purpose of winding up its affairs and liquidating its business.

(2) shareholders of stock with preferences in liquidation

rules.prose: Upon dissolution, corporate assets must be distributed first to creditors of the corporation, then to shareholders of stock with preferences in liquidation, and then to the other remaining shareholders of stock.

rules.prompt: Conflict of interest — corporate counsel advising a director or shareholder
core: A lawyer for the corporation who advises an individual director or shareholder has an actual conflict of interest.
(The duty to the corporation may conflict with the duty to the individual.)
May continue only if:
– he reasonably believes he can competently represent both interests (Model Rules), and
– he obtains informed written consent from the corporation AND the director/shareholder (Model Rules and CA Rules)

rules.prose: When a lawyer representing the corporation provides legal services or advice to an individual director or shareholder, this presents an actual conflict of interest because the attorney owes a duty to the corporation that may conflict with his duty to the director or shareholder. The attorney can only continue the representation if he reasonably believes he can competently represent the corporation's interests and the director or shareholder's interests, and he obtains informed written consent from both the corporation and the director or shareholder.
rules.tip: Business Associations essays frequently include professional responsibility issues; conflict of interest is the most common.
rules.subject: Corporations

rules.prompt: Distributions — board discretion, insolvency limit, director liability
core: Distribution: a cash dividend or share buyback (a stock dividend or stock split is NOT a distribution).
– The board may authorize a dividend in its discretion
– A SH cannot compel a distribution unless the board acts in bad faith and abuses its discretion
– NO distribution if the corporation is insolvent or it would cause insolvency — equity test or balance sheet test, at the time declared
– Directors personally liable to the corporation for an unlawful distribution beyond the lawful amount

rules.prose: A corporation can declare a cash dividend or buy back shares; a stock dividend or split is not considered a distribution. In its discretion, the board may authorize payment of a dividend, and a shareholder cannot compel the board to authorize a distribution unless it acts in bad faith and abuses its discretion. A corporation may not make a distribution if it is insolvent or the distribution would cause such, determined under either the equity test or balance sheet test at the time the dividend is declared. Directors are personally liable to the corporation for an unlawful distribution beyond the lawful amount.
rules.subject: Corporations

rules.prompt: Shareholder derivative suit — standing, demand, recovery (vs direct)
core: Direct suit: recover damages for a breach of duty owed to the SH individually, causing direct harm.
Derivative suit: SH sues on behalf of the corporation to vindicate the corporation's rights.
– Standing: SH at the time of the wrong (or acquired shares from a SH who was) + maintain ownership throughout the litigation
– Must fairly and adequately represent the interests of the corporation
– Demand: written demand to the board → wait 90 days before commencing the action
– UNLESS the board rejects it earlier (tested against the business judgment rule) or waiting would cause irreparable injury; some states excuse a futile demand
– Recovery goes to the CORPORATION; SH may seek attorney's fees if the action produced a substantial benefit to the corporation

rules.prose: A shareholder may bring a direct action for a breach of duty owed to the shareholder individually, or a derivative action on behalf of the corporation to vindicate its rights. For standing, the plaintiff must be a shareholder at the time of the wrong or acquire the shares from one who was, maintain ownership throughout the litigation, and fairly and adequately represent the corporation's interests. The shareholder must make written demand to the board and wait 90 days before commencing the action, unless the board rejects it earlier or waiting would cause irreparable injury; some states excuse a futile demand. Any recovery goes to the corporation.
rules.subject: Corporations

rules.prompt: LLC — formation, management, fiduciary duties, liability shield
core: LLC: members get limited liability (like a corporation) with flexible management and pass-through taxation (like a partnership).
– Created by filing ARTICLES OF ORGANIZATION with the state; name must include "LLC" or "limited liability company"
– Member-managed (presumed): members have broad authority to bind the LLC
– Manager-managed: members do NOT have authority to bind the LLC
– Fiduciary duties: owed by members to each other (as in a partnership) and to the entity (as in a corporation)
– An operating agreement controls over statutory provisions

– C b f A O O w t s; n m i "L" o "l l c"
– M-m (p): m h b a t b t L

rules.prose: A limited liability company provides its investors, called members, with limited liability like a corporation, yet flexible management and pass-through taxation like a partnership. An LLC is created by filing articles of organization with the state, and the name must include "LLC" or the words "limited liability company." Management is presumed by all members but can be centralized in managers: members have broad authority to bind a member-managed LLC but not a manager-managed LLC. Fiduciary duties are owed by members to each other, as in a partnership, and to the entity itself, as in a corporation.
rules.tip: Favored LLC issues: duty of loyalty (apply the director/officer fiduciary-duty rules), piercing the veil of limited liability (usually the alter-ego theory), and dissolution.
rules.subject: Corporations

core: Agency law addresses the legal consequences of one person (the agent) acting on behalf of, and subject to the control of, another person (the principal).

rules.subject: Agency

core: – Principal: typically an employer, such as a corporation or a partnership
– Agent: of a corporation — a director, officer, or other employee; of a partnership — a partner or employee
– Independent contractors can be agents if they are subject to control over their physical conduct by the principal

rules.prose: The principal is typically an employer, such as a corporation or a partnership. The agent of a corporation is typically a director, officer, or other employee, and the agent of a partnership is typically a partner or employee. Independent contractors can be agents if they are subject to control over their physical conduct by the principal.
rules.subject: Agency

rules.section: Agency — Contractual Liability of the Principal
core: A principal is liable on a contract the agent enters into on the principal's behalf if the agent has the power to bind the principal.
The agent has the power to bind the principal when the agent has actual authority or apparent authority.
bare: A p i l o a c t a e i o t p b i t a h t p t b t p.

rules.prose: A principal is subject to liability on a contract that the agent enters into on the principal's behalf if the agent has the power to bind the principal to the contract. An agent has the power to bind the principal to the contract when the agent has actual authority or apparent authority.
rules.tip: Contractual liability of the principal is frequently tested. Discuss ALL theories of authority to earn full credit, spending more time on the theory or theories that are relevant based on the facts.
rules.subject: Agency
rules.area: Liability of Principal and Agent to Third Parties

rules.prose: Express actual authority for an agent to enter into a contract on behalf of the principal can be created via oral or written words; clear, direct, and definite language; or specific detailed terms and instructions.
rules.subject: Agency
rules.area: Liability of Principal and Agent to Third Parties

rules.section: Agency — Contractual Liability of the Principal

core: Allows the agent to take whatever actions are properly necessary to achieve the principal's objectives,
based on the agent's reasonable understanding of the manifestations and objectives of the principal.
By position: an agent placed in a position that customarily has certain authority (e.g., treasurer) has implied authority to carry out its duties.

rules.prose: Implied actual authority allows an agent to take whatever actions are properly necessary to achieve the principal's objectives, based on the agent's reasonable understanding of the manifestations and objectives of the principal. If the agent is placed in a position that customarily has certain authority, such as treasurer, the agent will have implied authority to carry out the duties of that position.
rules.tip: Past CA bar essays have presented agents placed in positions such as president, vice president, salesperson, and technology person — these positions all carry implied authority to carry out certain duties.
rules.subject: Agency
rules.area: Liability of Principal and Agent to Third Parties

rules.section: Agency — Contractual Liability of the Principal
rules.prompt: Apparent authority — rule and reasonable belief
core: The principal causes a third party to reasonably believe the agent has authority to act → the principal is bound to the contract.
Reasonable belief — a court considers:
– trade customs and industry standards
– the agent's position: appointment to a specific position (e.g., vice president, treasurer) manifests the customary level of authority of that position

rules.prose: Apparent authority results when a principal causes a third party to reasonably believe that the agent has authority to act; if the third party reasonably believes the agent has authority, the principal will be bound to the contract. To determine whether the third party's belief is reasonable, a court will consider trade customs and industry standards. The agent's position will also be considered — if the agent is appointed to a specific position, such as vice president or treasurer, the principal will be found to have made a manifestation that the agent has the customary level of authority possessed by a person in that position.
rules.tip: Positions such as president, vice president, salesperson, and technology person will likely cause a third party to reasonably believe the agent has apparent authority to act on behalf of the principal.
rules.subject: Agency
rules.area: Liability of Principal and Agent to Third Parties

rules.section: Agency — Contractual Liability of the Principal
core: A principal can ratify (affirm/approve) an agent's unauthorized act and thereby be bound to the contract. Requires:
(1) ratification of the ENTIRE contract or transaction, by express assent or conduct that indicates affirmation
(2) timely ratification — before the third party withdraws from the contract
(3) the principal has knowledge of the material facts involved in the original act

rules.prose: A principal can ratify an act performed by an agent, even if the agent did not have authority to act, and therefore be bound to a contract with a third party. The principal must ratify the entire contract or transaction by express assent or conduct that indicates affirmation, the ratification must be timely — before the third party withdraws from the contract — and the principal must have knowledge of the material facts involved in the original act.
rules.subject: Agency
rules.area: Liability of Principal and Agent to Third Parties

core: A principal can be vicariously liable AND directly liable to a third party who is harmed by a tort committed by an agent.

rules.subject: Agency
rules.area: Liability of Principal and Agent to Third Parties

rules.section: Agency — Tort Liability of the Principal
rules.prompt: Respondeat superior — rule and scope of employment
core: A principal may be vicariously liable for a tort committed by an agent acting within the scope of his employment.
– Agent: an employee, director, officer, or partner
– Scope of employment: performing work assigned by the employer; mistakes or careless acts while performing assigned duties are still within the scope

rules.prose: A principal may be vicariously liable for a tort committed by an agent acting within the scope of his employment; the agent can be an employee, director, officer, or partner. An agent is acting within the scope of employment if she is performing work assigned by the employer, and an agent who makes a mistake or acts carelessly while performing her assigned duties is still acting within her scope of employment.
rules.subject: Agency
rules.area: Liability of Principal and Agent to Third Parties

rules.section: Agency — Tort Liability of the Principal
rules.prompt: Respondeat superior — intentional torts and work-related travel
core: Intentional torts may be within the scope of duty if:
– committed during work hours
– the agent was motivated to act for the principal's benefit, and
– the act was within her assigned duties
Traveling between work and home: usually NOT within the scope of employment.
Travel for work purposes (business trips, visiting clients): within the scope.

rules.prose: Intentional torts may be in the scope of duty if they occur during work hours, the agent was motivated to act for the principal's benefit, and the act was within her assigned duties. Traveling between work and home is usually not within the scope of employment, but travel for work purposes, such as business trips and visiting clients, is within the scope of employment.
rules.subject: Agency
rules.area: Liability of Principal and Agent to Third Parties

rules.section: Agency — Tort Liability of the Principal
core: A principal may be directly liable to a third party harmed by an agent's conduct if the principal:
– authorizes or ratifies the agent's conduct
– is negligent in hiring or supervising the agent, or
– delegates a nondelegable duty to the agent

rules.prose: A principal may also be directly liable to a third party harmed by an agent's conduct if the principal authorizes or ratifies the agent's conduct, is negligent in hiring or supervising the agent, or delegates a nondelegable duty to the agent.
rules.subject: Agency
rules.area: Liability of Principal and Agent to Third Parties

core: A partnership is an association of 2 or more persons to carry on a for-profit business as co-owners.
– Sharing of profits: the KEY test — if profits from the business are shared, a partnership is presumed
– Intent: specific intent to form a partnership is NOT necessary
– Agreement: only the agreement to conduct a for-profit business as co-owners; NO writing required
bare: A p i a a o 2 o m p t c o a f-p b a c-o.
– S o p: t K t — i p f t b a s, a p i p
– I: s i t f a p i N n

rules.prose: A partnership is an association of two or more persons to carry on a for-profit business as co-owners. The key test to determine whether a partnership exists is whether there is a sharing of profits from the business; if so, a partnership will be presumed. It is not necessary that people have the specific intent to form a partnership, and the only agreement required is the agreement to conduct a for-profit business as co-owners — no writing is required.
rules.subject: Partnerships

rules.section: Partnership — Duties and Rights Between Partners
rules.prompt: Partner as agent for the partnership
core: A partner is an agent of the partnership (the principal) for business purposes.
The partner can contractually bind the partnership when acting with actual (express or implied) or apparent authority.

rules.prose: A partner is an agent of the partnership, the principal, for business purposes, and can contractually bind the partnership when the partner acts with actual (express or implied) or apparent authority. As an agent, the partner can commit the partnership to binding contracts with third parties.

rules.prompt: Partner's apparent authority (and how it is cut off)
core: A partner performs an unauthorized act in the ORDINARY COURSE of partnership business + the third party reasonably believes the partner has authority → apparent authority.
– Third party KNOWS the partner lacked authority → cannot assert apparent authority to bind the partnership
– Partnership sends written notice of the partner's lack of authority and the third party receives it → no apparent authority

rules.prose: If a partner performs an unauthorized act in the ordinary course of partnership business and the third party reasonably believes the partner has authority to act, there will be apparent authority. If the third party has knowledge that the partner lacked authority, it cannot assert apparent authority and bind the partnership to a contract. If the partnership sends written notice to the third party indicating the partner's lack of authority and the third party receives the notice, there will be no apparent authority.

rules.prompt: Agent's liability on the contract — disclosed, unidentified, undisclosed principal
core: Whether A is also liable to the 3P depends upon P's status:
– DISCLOSED principal (3P had notice + knew P's identity) → A is NOT liable
– PARTIALLY DISCLOSED (unidentified) principal (3P had notice but not P's identity) → A is liable
– UNDISCLOSED principal (3P did not know of the agency relationship at all) → A is liable
– Warranty of authority: breached if A lacks the power to bind P → A liable for damages
bare: W A i a l t t 3P d u P s:
– D p (3P h n + k P i) → A i N l
– P D (u) p (3P h n b n P i) → A i l
– U p (3P d n k o t a r a a) → A i l
– W o a: b i A l t p t b P → A l f d
guided: W___ A is also l___ to the 3P d___ upon P___ s___:
– P___ D___ (u___) p___ (3P had n___ but not P___ i___) → A is l___
– U___ p___ (3P did not k___ of the a___ r___ at all) → A is l___
– W___ of a___: b___ if A l___ the p___ to b___ P → A l___ for d___
rules.prose: Whether an agent is also liable to the third party depends upon the principal's status. If the third party had notice and knew the principal's identity, the principal is disclosed and the agent is not liable. If the third party had notice but did not know the principal's identity, the principal is partially disclosed (unidentified) and the agent is liable. If the third party did not know of the agency relationship at all, the principal is undisclosed and the agent is liable. The agent also breaches the warranty of authority if she lacks the power to bind the principal and is liable for damages.
rules.subject: Agency
rules.area: Liability of Principal and Agent to Third Parties

rules.prompt: Duties owed between agent and principal — 4 each
core: Agent owes P:
– LOYALTY: work only for P's benefit — no competing, usurping P's business opportunity, or secret profits
– REASONABLE CARE: perform with reasonable diligence and skill
– OBEDIENCE: obey reasonable instructions
– NOTIFICATION and ACCOUNTING: keep P informed; account for funds

Principal owes A: compensation (per express or implied agreement); fair dealing and good faith (safe work environment); cooperation (no interference); indemnification for losses not from A's wrongful conduct
P's remedies for breach include recovery of secret profits and forfeiture of compensation

rules.prose: The agent owes duties of loyalty (work only for the principal's benefit; no competing, usurping the principal's business opportunity, or taking secret profits), reasonable care (perform with reasonable diligence and skill), obedience (obey reasonable instructions), and notification and accounting (keep the principal informed and account for funds). The principal owes duties to compensate per express or implied agreement, to deal fairly and in good faith and provide a safe work environment, to cooperate and not interfere with the agent's work, and to indemnify and reimburse losses not resulting from the agent's wrongful conduct. The principal's remedies for breach include recovery of secret profits and forfeiture of compensation.

rules.section: Causes of Action and Types of Remedies
rules.prompt: Legal vs. equitable vs. restitution-based remedies — definitions
core: – Legal remedies: money damages
– Equitable remedies: court-ordered remedies (e.g., injunctions, specific performance)
– Restitution-based remedies: based on unjust enrichment; include both legal and equitable remedies

– E r: c-o r (e., i, s p)

rules.prose: "Legal remedies" generally refers to money damages, and "equitable remedies" generally refers to court-ordered remedies, such as injunctions and specific performance. Restitution-based remedies are based on unjust enrichment and include legal and equitable remedies.
rules.tip: Discuss both legal remedies and equitable remedies (including restitution-based remedies) unless the question clearly limits you to one type.
rules.subject: Remedies

core: – Torts: compensate the plaintiff for harm or injury — pain and suffering, medical expenses, lost wages
– Contracts: expectation damages — put the injured party in the same position as if the contract was performed
– Expectation amount generally based on the contract price for performance and the fair market value of performance

rules.prose: In torts, compensatory damages compensate a plaintiff for harm or injury, including pain and suffering, medical expenses, and lost wages. In contracts, compensatory damages most often take the form of expectation damages and are intended to put the injured party in the same position as if the contract was performed. Generally, the amount of expectation damages will be based on the contract price for performance and the fair market value of performance.
rules.tip: Always discuss compensatory damages. Use the specific dollar amounts provided in the question and explain the math (addition/subtraction) used to determine the award.

core: Reasonably foreseeable damages (beyond expectation damages) related to the tort or breach (e.g., loss of profit). To recover:
– Foreseeability: natural and probable consequences, OR actually contemplated when the contract was formed
– Causation: caused by the defendant's tort or breach
– Certainty: dollar amount proven with reasonable certainty (new business → too speculative; no award)

rules.prose: Consequential damages are reasonably foreseeable damages, other than expectation damages, that are related to the tort or breach of contract, such as loss of profit. To recover, the damages must be the natural and probable consequences of the tort or breach of contract or actually contemplated by the parties at the time the contract was formed; the plaintiff must show that the damages were caused by the defendant's tort or breach — damages that would have occurred anyway are not recoverable; and the plaintiff must prove the dollar amount with reasonable certainty — when the amount is too speculative, as with a new business, the court will not award consequential damages.

rules.prose: A TRO is the first form of injunctive relief the plaintiff will seek, and it is usually issued to maintain the status quo until a preliminary injunction hearing is held. A TRO is issued for a short period of time — a maximum of 14 days under the federal rules and 15 days under the California rules — and it does not require notice to the defendant or an opportunity to be heard.
rules.tip: Unless the facts indicate that the California rules apply (the case is brought in California Superior Court), assume the federal rules apply.

core: – Irreparable harm if not issued (money damages adequate → no irreparable harm)
– Balancing the hardships: plaintiff's hardship if denied outweighs the defendant's (and third parties') if issued
– Likelihood of success on the merits at trial
– Enforceability: feasible for the court to enforce

rules.prose: To obtain a TRO or a preliminary injunction, the plaintiff must show that he will suffer irreparable harm if the injunction is not issued — if money damages would be adequate, there is no irreparable harm, though damages are inadequate for real property and unique personal property; that his hardship if the injunction is not issued outweighs the hardship on the defendant and third parties if it is issued; and a likelihood of success on the merits. The request may also be denied if it is not feasible for the court to enforce the injunction; courts avoid injunctions requiring extensive involvement or continued supervision.
rules.tip: Thoroughly analyze both parties' hardship arguments (the defendant can cite hardship to third parties). For likelihood of success, discuss the underlying cause(s) of action and why the plaintiff is likely to win — briefly, if already analyzed earlier in the answer.
rules.subject: Remedies

– irreparable harm if the injunction is not granted, and
– balancing of the hardships: her hardships if not issued significantly greater than the hardships on the defendant and third parties if issued

rules.prose: After the plaintiff has won her case, a permanent injunction is granted to prevent the violation of the plaintiff's rights or to restore to the plaintiff rights that have been violated. The plaintiff must show irreparable harm if the injunction is not granted, and that her hardships if the injunction is not issued will be significantly greater than the hardships on the defendant and third parties if the injunction is issued.

rules.prompt: Specific performance — rule and six elements
core: Court orders the breaching party to perform the contract. Elements:
(1) valid contract (offer, acceptance, consideration)
(2) terms certain and clear enough for a court order
(3) conditions precedent satisfied (or excused) — performance now due
(4) money damages inadequate (usually unique items)

rules.prose: Under the remedy of specific performance, the nonbreaching party can ask a court to order the breaching party to perform the contract. There must be a valid contract; the terms of the contract must be certain enough to allow a court to make an order; the nonbreaching party must have satisfied any conditions precedent, so the breaching party's performance is now due; money damages must be inadequate; it must be feasible for the court to enforce and supervise the performance; and no defenses may exist.
rules.tip: Specific performance is frequently tested. Discuss ALL of the listed elements to earn full credit.

core: – Statute of Frauds: no writing signed by the party against whom enforcement is sought containing all essential elements
– Fraud: contract voidable if the plaintiff engaged in fraud or misrepresentation when forming the contract
– Unilateral mistake by defendant: mistake as to a material fact + plaintiff knew or should have known → contract never formed

rules.prose: A court will not grant specific performance if the breaching party can assert a viable defense. Certain contracts are not enforceable if they fail to satisfy the Statute of Frauds, which requires a writing that is signed by the party against whom enforcement is sought and contains all essential elements. A contract is voidable if the plaintiff engaged in fraud or misrepresentation when forming the contract. And if the defendant made a unilateral mistake as to a material fact and the plaintiff knew or should have known about the mistake, the defendant can argue that the contract was never formed.

– Unclean hands: the plaintiff himself engaged in unethical or immoral acts relating to the contract
(either → the court may deny equitable relief)

rules.prose: If the plaintiff waited an unreasonably long time to seek the equitable relief and the delay prejudiced the defendant, the court may deny the relief under laches. If the plaintiff himself engaged in unethical or immoral acts relating to the contract, the court may deny equitable relief for unclean hands.
rules.tip: After discussing all relevant equitable remedies (specific performance, rescission, reformation, constructive trust, equitable lien), discuss laches and unclean hands.

rules.prose: The plaintiff must return or deliver any of the benefits of the contract it has received from the defendant. In rescission at law, the plaintiff must return the benefit at the same time the action is filed; in equitable rescission, the plaintiff need not return the benefit to the defendant until after the lawsuit is concluded. Most states, including California, have eliminated this distinction.

– Mutual mistake as to the content or effect of a writing: court may rewrite the contract
– Unilateral mistake: reformation allowed if the other party knew of the mistake and fraudulently induced it (or did nothing to correct it)

rules.prose: Reformation is the judicial rewriting of a contract or other document to reflect the parties' true intent. If both parties were mistaken as to the content or effect of a writing, the court may rewrite the contract. If one party is mistaken as to the content or effect of a writing, reformation will be allowed if the other party is aware of the mistake and fraudulently induced the mistake or does nothing to correct it.

rules.prose: A constructive trust is a judicially created remedy that imposes a duty to convey specific property to the plaintiff if the defendant would be unjustly enriched by retaining the property. The defendant must have committed a tort or breach of duty such that his retention of the property would unjustly enrich him; the defendant must hold title to the property; and the legal remedy of money damages must be inadequate.

– Tracing: plaintiff can trace his property from its original form to its current form (money → house: recover the house)

rules.prose: The defendant must have title for the plaintiff to seek a constructive trust. If the defendant transfers title to a third party, a constructive trust can be sought against the third party unless the third party is a bona fide purchaser — a purchaser for value who takes the property without knowledge of the defendant's wrongdoing. A plaintiff can trace his property from its original form to its current form; for example, a plaintiff can trace his money to the purchase of a house and recover the house from the defendant.

rules.prose: If the value of the property increased during the defendant's ownership, the plaintiff gets the property and the increase in value; however, the defendant is not liable to the plaintiff if the property decreased in value. If a constructive trust over an item of property is granted, the plaintiff's claim will be deemed fully satisfied, and the plaintiff cannot sue for additional damages.
rules.tip: If the property taken has increased in value, the plaintiff wants a constructive trust (entitled to the increase); if it has decreased, he does not (stuck with the decreased value).

rules.prose: If the plaintiff's money is used to purchase property, the plaintiff is entitled only to the amount of money that the defendant received from the plaintiff, and not to any increase in the value of the property. If the plaintiff forces a sale and the sale price does not cover the money owed, the plaintiff can seek the additional money from the defendant — whereas under a constructive trust theory, the plaintiff takes the property as full satisfaction of the claim and cannot seek additional money.

rules.section: Challenging the Validity of a Trust
core: The contestant must show four elements:
– Susceptibility: the testator was susceptible to being influenced
– Motive: the influencer has reason to benefit
– Opportunity: the influencer had opportunity to influence
– Causation: the influencer caused an UNNATURAL result

rules.prose: Under the traditional approach, a contestant must show four elements: susceptibility, meaning the testator was susceptible to being influenced; motive, meaning the influencer has reason to benefit; opportunity, meaning the influencer had the opportunity to influence; and causation, meaning the influencer caused an unnatural result.
rules.subject: Trusts

rules.section: Challenging the Validity of a Trust
core: A presumption of undue influence arises when:
– the principal beneficiary has a confidential relationship with the testator (e.g., parent/child, attorney/client)
– the principal beneficiary participated in executing the will, and
– the gift to the beneficiary is unnatural
Presumption arises → the beneficiary must show by CLEAR AND CONVINCING evidence that he did not exercise undue influence.
If undue influence was exerted → the beneficiary receives only his intestate entitlement (a friend takes nothing; a family member takes an intestate share).

rules.prose: A presumption of undue influence arises when the principal beneficiary of the will has a confidential relationship with the testator, the principal beneficiary participated in executing the will, and the gift to the beneficiary is unnatural. If a presumption arises, the beneficiary must show through clear and convincing evidence that he did not exercise undue influence. If the beneficiary exerted undue influence, he will only receive what he would be entitled to under an intestate approach — a friend of the testator would receive nothing because friends do not take under intestacy, while a family member would take an intestate share.

rules.prose: In California, trusts are presumed to be revocable. With a revocable trust, the settlor has the right to modify or terminate the trust. An irrevocable trust may be modified or terminated while the settlor is still alive only with the consent of all beneficiaries, and only if the proposed change will not interfere with a primary purpose of the trust.

– by the court: purpose becomes illegal, impracticable, or impossible
Trustee's own power to terminate: NONE, unless express termination provisions.

rules.prose: A trust may terminate automatically when the trust purpose has been accomplished; if the settlor is deceased or has no remaining interest and all the beneficiaries consent, although a trustee can block a premature termination by the beneficiaries if the trust is shown to have an unfulfilled material purpose; or by the court if the purpose becomes illegal, impracticable, or impossible. The trustee has no power to terminate unless the trust contains express termination provisions.

rules.prose: When the trustee personally engages in a transaction involving trust property, a conflict of interest arises between the trustee's duties to the beneficiaries and her own personal interest. rules.prose: A resulting trust is implied by law to avoid unjust enrichment and requires the trustee to convey the property back to the settlor upon the failure of an express trust, where the presumed intent of the settlor is for the trustee to convey the property back, or upon an incomplete disposition of trust assets, where property left over after the trust is fulfilled goes back to the settlor.

– Must prove by CLEAR AND CONVINCING evidence
– Transferred to a 3P who is NOT a BFP → beneficiary may trace and recover the property, its proceeds, or other property received

rules.prose: A constructive trust is an equitable remedy to avoid unjust enrichment caused by wrongful conduct, requiring the trustee to convey the property. The defendant must hold title to the property, and the wrongful conduct that caused the settlor to convey the property includes homicide, theft, or conversion, fraud or undue influence, and breach of fiduciary duty; it must be proved by clear and convincing evidence. If the subject matter has been transferred to a third party who is not a bona fide purchaser, the beneficiary may trace and recover the property, its proceeds, or other property received. Defenses are unclean hands and laches.

– The trust must be in WRITING and IDENTIFIED in the will
– The trust may be revocable — but if the trust is revoked, the pour-over provision FAILS

rules.prose: A pour-over gift occurs when a will directs that property be distributed to a trust upon the happening of an event, and it allows probate to be avoided because the trust is funded by the will. The trust must be in writing and identified in the will. The trust may be revocable, but if it is revoked, the pour-over provision fails.
rules.subject: Trusts

rules.prompt: Secret vs semi-secret trusts — proof and remedies
core: SECRET trust: an apparently outright gift in a will actually based on the devisee's promise to T to hold the property for another's benefit.
– Proof: CLEAR AND CONVINCING evidence → remedy is a CONSTRUCTIVE trust (avoids unjust enrichment of the secret trustee)
SEMI-SECRET trust: the will directs a gift be held in trust WITHOUT naming a beneficiary.
– C/L: extrinsic evidence barred → the gift FAILS; remedy is a RESULTING trust for the testator's heirs
– Modern trend: impose a CONSTRUCTIVE trust in favor of the intended beneficiary, if known

– P: C A C e → r i a C t (a u e o t s t)

rules.prose: A secret trust exists when an apparently outright gift in a will rests on the devisee's promise to the testator to hold the property for another's benefit; proof requires clear and convincing evidence, and the remedy is a constructive trust to avoid unjust enrichment of the secret trustee. A semi-secret trust exists when a will directs a gift be held in trust without naming a beneficiary; the common law prevents extrinsic evidence to prove the trust, so the gift fails, and the remedy is a resulting trust in favor of the testator's heirs. The modern trend imposes a constructive trust for the intended beneficiary, if known.
